item.title: How to Build Your Own Table When There's No Seat for You in Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What if waiting for someone else to make room for you is actually the biggest leadership mistake you'll ever make?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I knew I had to have Neetu Sadhwani as my very first guest because her story mirrors what so many of us have felt but were afraid to voice. She walked away from everything that looked like success on paper to build something that actually mattered to her.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Building your own table is more powerful than waiting for someone else to offer you a seat.
  2. Leaving comfort behind for purpose requires courage, but it's essential for authentic leadership.
  3. Burnout is often a signal that you're not aligned with your true purpose and values.
  4. Grace, grit, and vision are the three pillars needed to build something meaningful from scratch.
  5. Believing in yourself even when it's hard is a non-negotiable skill for women leaders.
  6. Real balance isn't about perfect equilibrium but about making intentional choices aligned with your priorities.
  7. Women are more capable than they think, but permission-giving starts from within.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Why waiting for a seat at the table just isn't it anymore — she built her own"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're sitting in yet another meeting, watching decisions get made around you instead of with you? Neetu felt it too. Despite climbing the corporate ladder and checking all the traditional success boxes, she found herself burning out in spaces that weren't designed for someone like her to truly thrive. So she did what most people are too scared to do—she stopped waiting for an invitation and built her own table instead.

Here's what I love about Neetu's approach: she didn't just quit in a blaze of glory and hope for the best. She recognized that burnout wasn't just about being tired—it was her internal compass screaming that she was living misaligned with her true purpose and values. When you're constantly fighting for scraps of recognition or trying to squeeze yourself into spaces that don't celebrate who you are, that's not a you problem. That's a signal that it's time to create something new.

We talked about the three pillars that actually matter when you're building from scratch: grace, grit, and vision. Grace to forgive yourself for staying too long in the wrong rooms. Grit to keep going when building your own thing feels impossibly hard. And vision to see possibilities that don't exist yet. The truth is, you already have everything you need—the question is whether you're willing to bet on yourself even when it's terrifying.

The biggest lie we've been sold is that balance means having everything perfectly weighted all the time. Real balance is about making intentional choices that align with what matters most to you right now. Sometimes that means saying no to good opportunities so you can say yes to great ones you're creating yourself. Sometimes it means disappointing people who've gotten comfortable with a version of you that's too small.

If you've been waiting for someone to tap you on the shoulder and invite you to lead, this conversation will give you the permission you've been looking for to stop waiting and start building. Want to discover your own leadership blind spots and find out if you're ready to build your own table?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Neetu Sadhwani

Neetu Sadhwani is a visionary entrepreneur and leader who exemplifies building something meaningful from the ground up. Known for her courage in leaving comfort behind to chase purpose, she's a powerful voice on creating your own opportunities rather than waiting for permission.

item.title: How to Build Stronger Relationships Through Emotional Wellness and Boundaries
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that most of your relationship struggles aren't about the other person at all?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot lately about how we show up in our relationships—with our teams, our partners, our kids. When licensed therapist Vanessa Morgan walked me through the real work of emotional wellness, I realized I'd been approaching so many of my connections all wrong.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Emotional wellness requires intentional management and grace toward yourself as you navigate life's complexities.
  2. Setting healthy boundaries is essential for maintaining strong relationships in both personal and professional contexts.
  3. Effective communication skills can be learned and applied to resolve conflicts more constructively.
  4. Building deeper connections requires vulnerability and the willingness to engage authentically with others.
  5. Conflict resolution is a skill that strengthens relationships when approached with the right tools and mindset.
  6. Personal growth and emotional intelligence directly impact your ability to lead and influence others.
  7. Professional therapy insights can be practically applied to everyday relationship challenges.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Managing your emotional world with grace and intention is not just possible—it's essential for thriving relationships."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're juggling a million things and someone asks for just one more favor, and suddenly you're snapping at people you actually care about? Or when conflict arises and you find yourself either shutting down completely or going into full battle mode? Here's what Vanessa taught me: those moments aren't character flaws—they're data points telling you exactly where your boundaries need work.

The conversation we had about emotional wellness completely shifted how I think about relationship management. Vanessa broke down why most of us struggle with boundaries not because we don't want them, but because we've never learned how to set them without feeling guilty or selfish. She walked me through practical strategies that work whether you're dealing with a difficult board member or a teenager who keeps pushing every limit you set. The key isn't becoming harder or more distant—it's becoming more intentional about how you engage.

What really got me was her insight about conflict resolution. Most of us treat conflict like a fire that needs to be put out immediately, but Vanessa explained how the right approach to disagreement actually strengthens relationships. She shared specific communication techniques that help you stay grounded when emotions run high, and how vulnerability—the real kind, not the performative type—creates the deeper connections we're all craving.

The professional applications here are massive. When you understand how to manage your own emotional responses and communicate with clarity, you show up as a different kind of leader. Your team feels safer bringing you problems. Your partnerships become more productive. Even your family dynamics shift when you stop trying to control outcomes and start focusing on how you show up in each interaction.

This isn't about perfection or having it all figured out—it's about building skills that compound over time. Vanessa's background as a licensed therapist means she's not just sharing theory; she's giving you tools that work in real life, with real people, in real messy situations. If you've been feeling stuck in patterns that aren't serving you, or if you're ready to build the kind of relationships that actually energize you instead of draining you, you need to dive into the full conversation and take our relationship wellness assessment.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Vanessa Morgan

Vanessa Morgan is a licensed marriage and family therapist with extensive professional experience in communication, conflict resolution, and relationship dynamics. She specializes in helping individuals and couples build deeper, more meaningful connections in both personal and professional settings.

item.title: How Intuitive Leadership Transforms Motherhood and Business Success
item.content:encoded:
What if the secret to leading powerfully isn't in your head, but in trusting your gut?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, Hey friend, I had one of those conversations that left me buzzing with possibility. Nikki Bostwick, founder of The Fullest, dropped so much wisdom about intuitive leadership that I'm still processing it all. This episode is pure permission-giving gold for anyone juggling motherhood and big business dreams.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Intuitive leadership requires trusting your inner wisdom over external expectations in both business and parenting decisions.
  2. Building a wellness brand authentically means creating products and content that solve problems you personally experience.
  3. Balancing entrepreneurship and motherhood isn't about perfect harmony but about conscious choices and self-compassion.
  4. Heart-centered business approaches can lead to deeper connections with customers and more sustainable success.
  5. There's no single right way to manage multiple roles - you have permission to do it your own way.
  6. Wellness practices like mindful morning routines can anchor busy mothers and business leaders throughout chaotic days.
  7. Community and honest conversation about the messy realities of modern womanhood create powerful support systems.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The messy, beautiful truth about doing it all is that you get to do it your own way."

The big idea

You know that voice inside you that whispers what feels right? The one you've been taught to question, analyze, and second-guess? Nikki Bostwick is here to tell you it's time to start listening. As the founder of The Fullest, a wellness brand that's redefining what it means to live fully, Nikki has built her entire business—and approach to motherhood—on trusting her intuition over external expectations.

Here's what I love about Nikki's approach: she's not preaching perfection. She's talking about the beautiful mess of doing it all your own way. When she shares how she navigates building a heart-centered business while raising kids, it's not about finding perfect balance. It's about making conscious choices and extending yourself the same compassion you'd give your best friend. She's created products and content that solve problems she personally faces, and that authenticity is what makes The Fullest so magnetic.

We dive deep into what intuitive leadership actually looks like in practice. It's about trusting your inner wisdom when making business decisions, even when the market research says something different. It's about parenting from your gut instead of the latest expert advice. And yes, it's about building those wellness practices—like mindful morning routines—that anchor you when everything feels chaotic.

What struck me most was Nikki's reminder that there's no single right way to manage multiple roles. You have permission to do it your way. The community she's building around honest conversations about modern womanhood? It's proof that when we stop pretending everything is perfect, we create the most powerful support systems. This conversation will have you questioning everything you thought you knew about leadership, and I can't wait for you to hear what Nikki shared about the moment she stopped following everyone else's playbook.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Nikki Bostwick

Nikki Bostwick is the founder and editor-in-chief of The Fullest, a wellness brand known for its beloved saffron latte. She's a wellness visionary and mother who brings a heart-centered approach to both business and parenting, emphasizing intuitive leadership in all aspects of life.

item.title: How a 26-Year-Old Single Mom Is Crushing It in Tech: Hannah White's Story
item.content:encoded:
What if being a single mom at 26 actually makes you a better tech leader?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I met Hannah White and immediately knew her story needed to be shared. Here's a young woman who's rewriting the script on what success looks like in your twenties, proving that life's unexpected turns can actually accelerate your professional growth.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Success in your twenties doesn't have to look like traditional career paths - you can build something meaningful while navigating life's unexpected turns.
  2. Single motherhood can actually strengthen your professional skills by forcing you to become more efficient, organized, and decisive.
  3. Technology and the right systems can be game-changers for managing both work responsibilities and family life effectively.
  4. Owning your power starts with accepting your current circumstances rather than waiting for perfect conditions to pursue your goals.
  5. Building a career in tech is possible at any life stage, even when balancing significant personal responsibilities.
  6. Setting boundaries between work and family time is crucial for maintaining both professional growth and quality parenting.
  7. Finding your identity as both a professional and a mother requires intentional choices about how you spend your time and energy.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't have to wait for the perfect moment to own your power - you can start exactly where you are right now."

The big idea

When Hannah White became a single mom in her early twenties, she could have easily bought into the narrative that her career dreams were over. Instead, she did something remarkable: she used the skills motherhood demanded of her—efficiency, organization, decisive problem-solving—to build a thriving career in IT. At 26, she's proving that single motherhood isn't a career limitation; it's a masterclass in operational excellence.

What struck me most about Hannah's approach is how she refuses to wait for perfect conditions. While other people her age are still figuring out their five-year plans, she's already mastered the art of making strategic decisions under pressure. She's learned that owning your power isn't about having all your ducks in a row—it's about accepting where you are and building from that foundation. Her story challenges everything we've been told about needing to choose between family and career advancement.

Hannah gets refreshingly real about the systems that keep her sane: the technology tools that streamline her workflow, the boundary-setting that protects both her work performance and her presence with her boys, and the mindset shifts that transformed her from feeling behind her peers to recognizing she's actually ahead of the curve. She's discovered that the same skills that make her an effective mother—anticipating problems, managing multiple priorities, staying calm under pressure—are exactly what make her invaluable in the tech world.

This conversation will shift how you think about timing, circumstances, and what it really means to build a meaningful career. Hannah's proving that success in your twenties doesn't have to follow traditional timelines, and that sometimes life's biggest challenges become your greatest competitive advantages. Ready to discover how your current circumstances might be the exact foundation you need to build something extraordinary? Dive into the full conversation and take our quiz to identify which of Hannah's strategies will work best for your situation.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Hannah White

Hannah White is a 26-year-old IT professional at Tomahawk and single mother of two boys. She's navigating the tech industry while balancing the demands of motherhood, demonstrating resilience and determination in building her career. Hannah is also Aimee Rickabus's stepdaughter, bringing a personal perspective to this candid conversation about modern motherhood and professional ambition.

item.title: How CrossFit Champion Kelly Abney Built Her Athletic Empire While Breaking Barriers
item.content:encoded:
Why are the strongest women in the room often the ones apologizing for taking up space?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking about this a lot lately—how we've been conditioned to shrink our power instead of wielding it. When I sat down with CrossFit Games champion Kelly Abney, I knew I was talking to someone who had figured out how to own her space without apology. Her story isn't just about athletic achievement; it's about redefining what strength looks like for women in every arena.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Success in athletics and business requires embracing both your strength and vulnerability as a woman.
  2. The unseen hustle behind women's achievements often involves navigating unique challenges that men don't face.
  3. Building an empire means creating systems that support your values while scaling your impact.
  4. Grit isn't just about pushing through—it's about strategic persistence aligned with your goals.
  5. Breaking barriers requires you to own your space unapologetically while lifting others up.
  6. Balance isn't about perfection—it's about intentional choices that align with your priorities.
  7. Your strength as a woman isn't something to minimize—it's your competitive advantage.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Your strength as a woman isn't something to minimize—it's your competitive advantage."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about breaking barriers: the hardest part isn't proving you belong—it's believing you deserve to take up space in the first place. Kelly Abney didn't just become a CrossFit Games champion by accident. She built an athletic empire by doing something most of us struggle with: she stopped apologizing for her strength and started leveraging it as her greatest competitive advantage.

The conversation we had reminded me why I'm so passionate about this work. Kelly shared how she navigated challenges that her male counterparts never even had to consider—from questioning whether being 'too strong' would make her less feminine, to building systems that honored her values while scaling her impact. She talked about the unseen hustle that goes into women's achievements, the behind-the-scenes work of proving yourself over and over again while making it look effortless.

What struck me most was how Kelly redefined grit. It's not about grinding yourself into the ground—it's about strategic persistence that actually aligns with your goals. She talked about balance not as some mythical perfect equation, but as making intentional choices about what matters most. And when she spoke about lifting others up while claiming her own space? That's when I knew this conversation would resonate with every woman trying to lead authentically.

The empire Kelly built wasn't just about personal achievement—it was about creating a model for other women to follow. She showed how embracing both your strength and vulnerability isn't a contradiction; it's the secret sauce that makes female leadership so powerful. If you've ever caught yourself minimizing your accomplishments or questioning whether you're 'too much,' this episode will give you the permission you've been waiting for to own your power completely.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kelly Abney

Kelly Abney is a CrossFit Games champion and founder of Abney Athletics. She's built a powerful presence in both competitive fitness and business, breaking barriers for women in athletics while creating her own empire.

item.title: From Grief to Grit: How This Widow Turned Loss Into Leadership Success
item.content:encoded:
What if the worst thing that ever happened to you became your greatest leadership asset?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I sat across from Leslie Christen and witnessed something extraordinary—a woman who transformed devastating loss into purposeful leadership. Her story challenged everything I thought I knew about resilience and reminded me why authentic conversations about grief and growth matter so much in our work.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The invisible labor of motherhood becomes even more complex when you're carrying it all alone after loss.
  2. Being underestimated in business can actually become your secret weapon for success.
  3. Technology and AI tools can be life-saving resources for overwhelmed mothers and entrepreneurs.
  4. Sisterhood and community support are essential for women rebuilding their lives after tragedy.
  5. Leading with soul and authenticity creates deeper connections than traditional business approaches.
  6. Resilience isn't about being strong all the time—it's about finding ways to keep moving forward.
  7. The patriarchal business world often undervalues women's emotional intelligence and heart-centered leadership.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Resilience isn't about being strong all the time—it's about finding ways to keep moving forward."

The big idea

When Leslie Christen became a widow and single mother, she faced a reality that millions of women know intimately but rarely discuss in business circles: carrying the invisible load alone. In our conversation, she shared how grief didn't just change her life—it revealed strengths she never knew she possessed and redefined how she shows up as a leader.

What struck me most about Leslie's journey wasn't just her resilience, but how she leveraged being underestimated. While others saw a grieving widow, she saw an opportunity to build something meaningful. She discovered that the emotional intelligence and heart-centered approach that the business world often dismisses as 'soft skills' were actually her competitive advantage. Her authentic leadership style created deeper connections with clients and team members than any traditional business strategy ever could.

Leslie also opened up about something we don't discuss enough—how technology and AI tools became lifelines in managing the overwhelming reality of solo parenting while building a business. She shared specific ways she streamlined her life and work, proving that embracing innovation isn't about replacing human connection, but creating space for what matters most. The sisterhood and community support she cultivated weren't just nice-to-haves; they became essential infrastructure for her success.

Perhaps most importantly, Leslie redefined what resilience actually looks like. It's not about maintaining strength every moment—it's about finding ways to keep moving forward, even when you're broken. Her story challenges the myth that effective leadership requires leaving your personal struggles at the door. Instead, she shows how integrating your whole self, including your pain and growth, creates more authentic and impactful leadership. If you've ever felt like your emotional approach to business makes you weak, Leslie's story will completely shift that narrative and give you permission to lead with your whole heart.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Leslie Christen

Leslie Christen is a powerhouse business owner, mother of two boys, and widow who has transformed personal loss into entrepreneurial strength. As one of the original founding members of the Office Hours Girls Club, she exemplifies how women can rise above grief and doubt to lead with both soul and strength.

item.title: How to Turn Dreams into Bold Goals and Live with Purpose as a Woman Leader
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest dreams are actually just goals waiting for a deadline and a strategy?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about the difference between dreaming and doing lately. When I sat down with Wendee Close, I knew I was talking to someone who'd figured out how to turn those late-night visions into morning action plans — all while raising kids and building something meaningful.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Bold goal-setting requires transforming dreams into specific, actionable steps that align with your values and purpose.
  2. Successful women leaders balance family and business by creating systems and being intentional about their time and energy.
  3. Supporting other women-owned businesses creates a powerful network that benefits everyone in the community.
  4. Living life on purpose means making decisions that align with your core values, even when it's challenging.
  5. WBENC certification opens doors for women entrepreneurs and creates opportunities for meaningful business connections.
  6. Motherhood and entrepreneurship can complement each other when you embrace both roles authentically.
  7. Setting boundaries around your goals helps you stay focused on what truly matters most.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"When you transform your dreams into actionable steps, you're not just setting goals—you're designing the life you want to live."

The big idea

You know that feeling when someone asks about your dreams and you immediately think 'but that's not realistic'? I used to live there too. But here's what Wendee Close taught me in our conversation: the gap between dreaming and achieving isn't about luck or timing — it's about getting brutally specific with what you want and then building the systems to support it.

The truth is, most of us have been conditioned to keep our dreams fluffy and safe. But Wendee showed me how she takes those big, beautiful visions and breaks them down into goals so clear that her calendar actually reflects her values. She's not just talking about time blocking (though that matters) — she's talking about making decisions that align with who you want to become, even when it's uncomfortable. Especially when it's uncomfortable.

What really struck me about our conversation was how she's navigating the intersection of motherhood and entrepreneurship without apologizing for either role. Too often, we're told we have to choose or that one has to suffer for the other to succeed. Wendee's approach is different — she's found ways to let these parts of her identity strengthen each other instead of competing for her energy.

We also dug deep into something that doesn't get talked about enough: how supporting other women in business isn't just nice to do, it's smart business strategy. Wendee shared specific examples of how investing in women-owned businesses and pursuing certifications like WBENC has created opportunities she never could have imagined. It's proof that when women succeed together, we all win bigger.

But here's what I want you to really hear — this isn't about perfection or having it all figured out. It's about getting intentional with your choices and brave with your boundaries. Wendee's story will challenge you to look at your own dreams differently and give you the practical tools to start moving on them today. Ready to find out which of your dreams are actually goals in disguise?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Wendee Close

Wendee Close is an entrepreneur, mother of two, and founder of Goals2Life.com. As a WBENC-certified business owner, she is passionate about empowering others to set bold goals and transform their dreams into actionable steps while supporting women-owned businesses across the country.

item.title: The Hidden Leadership Skills Every Woman Already Possesses
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you've been doing to keep your family running is actually world-class leadership training?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking about this for months—how we dismiss the incredible management work we do every single day. After juggling six kids, building a nine-figure company, and watching brilliant women undervalue their abilities, I knew it was time to have this conversation with you directly.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The invisible work women perform daily—organizing, coordinating, emotional labor—is actually sophisticated leadership in action.
  2. Leadership skills don't only develop in corporate boardrooms; they're honed through the complex demands of managing family life.
  3. Recognizing your existing leadership abilities is the first step to claiming your power in all areas of life.
  4. The juggling act of motherhood, marriage, and career teaches invaluable lessons about prioritization and decision-making.
  5. Women need to start giving themselves credit for the incredible management skills they demonstrate every single day.
  6. The journey from breakdown to breakthrough often begins with acknowledging the leadership you're already demonstrating.
  7. Self-growth and family life aren't competing priorities—they can fuel and strengthen each other when properly balanced.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The invisible work we do is leadership, and it's time we start treating it like it is."

The big idea

You wake up every morning and orchestrate a complex operation that would make Fortune 500 executives weep. You coordinate schedules, manage resources, navigate conflicts, make strategic decisions under pressure, and somehow keep everyone fed, clothed, and emotionally regulated. Yet when someone asks about your leadership experience, you probably say you don't have any.

Here's what I want you to understand: that invisible work you're doing? It's not just household management—it's sophisticated leadership in action. Every time you prioritize which crisis gets your attention first, you're making executive decisions. When you anticipate your teenager's needs before they melt down, you're demonstrating emotional intelligence that most CEOs pay consultants millions to develop. The way you juggle competing demands while keeping everyone's needs met? That's resource allocation and stakeholder management at its finest.

I learned this the hard way during my own journey from overwhelmed mother to confident CEO. For years, I felt like I was failing at everything because I couldn't see that managing my family's chaos was actually preparing me for the boardroom. The skills weren't separate—they were building on each other. The patience I developed with my six kids translated directly into better team management. The crisis management I mastered during family emergencies became invaluable during company pivots.

The shift happens when you stop seeing your daily juggling act as 'just' motherhood and start recognizing it as the leadership laboratory it actually is. You're not behind other women who went straight up the corporate ladder—you're developing a different, often more comprehensive skill set. The question isn't whether you have leadership abilities; it's whether you're ready to own them.

This episode dives deep into reframing your entire relationship with your capabilities, plus I'm sharing the specific moments that helped me recognize my own power—and how that recognition changed everything for my family and my business.

item.title: How a Leading Functional Medicine Doctor Balances Healing Others and Motherhood
item.content:encoded:
What if your gut health is secretly sabotaging your ability to show up as the leader you want to be?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been fascinated by the connection between our physical health and our leadership capacity, especially as working mothers juggling impossible schedules. When I sat down with Dr. Sunny Raleigh, I knew we'd uncover insights that could change how you think about caring for yourself while caring for everyone else.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The gut-brain connection plays a crucial role in healing chronic conditions and overall wellness.
  2. Distance parenting and co-parenting can work when you're intentional about showing up fully for your children.
  3. Small, consistent health changes create more impact than dramatic overhauls.
  4. Supporting concussion recovery requires a holistic approach that addresses both physical and neurological healing.
  5. You can run a successful medical practice while managing family responsibilities across multiple states.
  6. Treating patients that traditional medicine overlooks requires both medical expertise and deep compassion.
  7. Being a working mother means making tough choices but staying committed to both your mission and your family.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"I treat the patients no one else can—those suffering from conditions that traditional medicine often overlooks."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're running on fumes but pushing through anyway? Dr. Sunny Raleigh gets it. As a leading functional medicine doctor who's built a thriving practice while raising three boys across multiple states, she's cracked the code on something most of us are missing: the gut-brain connection that's either fueling your leadership or quietly undermining it.

Here's what blew my mind during our conversation—those digestive issues, brain fog, and energy crashes you've been writing off as 'just part of being busy' might actually be your body's way of telling you that your gut microbiome is out of balance. Dr. Raleigh explained how this connection impacts everything from your decision-making clarity to your emotional resilience, especially when you're managing the complex demands of leadership and motherhood simultaneously.

But this isn't another conversation about perfecting your morning routine or overhauling your entire life. Dr. Raleigh's approach is refreshingly practical—she's learned that small, consistent changes create more lasting impact than dramatic transformations. Whether she's treating patients that traditional medicine has overlooked or navigating distance parenting while building her practice, her philosophy centers on showing up fully in the moments that matter most.

What struck me most was how she's reframed the working mother narrative. Instead of seeing her medical mission and family responsibilities as competing priorities, she's found ways to honor both through intentional choices and boundaries that actually work. Her insights on co-parenting across state lines and building a practice that serves her values while supporting her family's needs offer a fresh perspective on what's possible when you stop trying to do everything perfectly and start doing the right things consistently.

The episode dives deep into actionable strategies for optimizing your gut health, supporting your brain function, and creating sustainable systems that serve both your leadership goals and your family's needs—plus there's a quiz at the end that will help you identify which area to focus on first.

Dr. Mary "Sunny" Raleigh is a renowned functional medicine doctor and founder of Restoration Healthcare in Irvine, California. item.title: How to Balance Creative Passion with Parenting: Lessons from Musician Stacy Clark
item.content:encoded:
What if supporting your children's dreams didn't require abandoning your own creative ambitions?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've wrestled with this tension myself—how do you pour into your kids' passions without losing sight of your own? My conversation with musician and advocate Stacy Clark opened my eyes to a completely different way of thinking about this balance. She's living proof that motherhood can actually amplify your creative work, not diminish it.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. You can maintain your creative identity while fully embracing motherhood and supporting your children's dreams.
  2. The entertainment industry requires strong advocacy skills when managing young actors and musicians.
  3. Music education is crucial for child development and deserves more support in our school systems.
  4. Purpose-driven work often emerges from personal experiences and challenges you've overcome.
  5. Balancing multiple roles requires clear boundaries and intentional time management.
  6. Supporting your children's careers doesn't mean sacrificing your own professional identity.
  7. Creative work can be a powerful tool for social change and community impact.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't have to choose between being a creative and being a mom—you can be both beautifully."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about being a creative mom: the world will try to convince you that supporting your children's dreams means sacrificing your own. Stacy Clark is here to shatter that myth completely. From performing on indie music stages to navigating Hollywood sets with her kids, she's mastered something most of us are still figuring out—how to be fully present for your children's journey while staying fiercely committed to your own creative path.

What struck me most about Stacy's approach is how she's turned her personal experiences into a powerful advocacy platform. When she saw gaps in music education, she didn't just complain—she became the solution. When her kids entered the entertainment industry, she didn't just hope for the best—she learned the business inside and out to protect and guide them. This is what purposeful leadership looks like: taking your challenges and transforming them into change that serves others.

The entertainment industry piece of her story particularly fascinated me because it mirrors what so many of us face in corporate America—environments that aren't always designed with families in mind. Stacy's insights about boundary-setting, advocacy, and maintaining your professional identity while championing your children apply whether you're managing young performers or simply trying to show up fully in your own career while raising kids who have big dreams of their own.

But here's the real game-changer from our conversation: Stacy's belief that your creative work can actually become more impactful when it's informed by your experience as a mother. Instead of compartmentalizing these roles, she's found ways to let them strengthen each other. Her music education advocacy grew directly from watching her children navigate their own creative journeys, and her parenting approach is deeply influenced by her understanding of what it takes to build a sustainable creative career.

This episode will challenge everything you think you know about balancing creative passion with parenting, and Stacy's practical strategies for time management and boundary-setting are ones you'll want to implement immediately—no matter what industry you're in or what stage of motherhood you're navigating.

item.title: From Hollywood Makeup Artist to Clean Beauty Pioneer: Laura Linsenmeyer's Story
item.content:encoded:
What if leaving your dream career was actually the first step toward building your legacy?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about the moment when security and purpose collide—when you realize that the safe path might be suffocating your soul. Laura Linsenmeyer's story hit me right in the gut because she walked away from a thriving Hollywood makeup career to build something that mattered more than her next paycheck.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Transitioning from a creative career to entrepreneurship requires courage to follow your values over security.
  2. Clean beauty isn't just about products—it's about creating a movement toward conscious living.
  3. Building a purpose-driven business means prioritizing community and values alongside profit.
  4. Motherhood can amplify your mission and drive you to create positive change in the world.
  5. Leading a woman-powered brand requires intentional cultivation of supportive relationships.
  6. Authenticity in business comes from aligning your personal values with your professional mission.
  7. Success in conscious entrepreneurship is measured by impact, not just revenue.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Clean beauty isn't just about what we put on our skin—it's about the values we choose to live by"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're successful by everyone else's standards but something inside you is screaming for more? Laura Linsenmeyer was living the dream as a Hollywood makeup artist, working with A-list celebrities and building a name for herself in an industry millions want to break into. But success felt hollow when she realized the products she was using every day were filled with chemicals she wouldn't want near her own family.

The transition from creative professional to clean beauty entrepreneur wasn't just about switching careers—it was about having the audacity to believe that business could be a force for good. Laura didn't just want to create another beauty brand; she wanted to spark a movement toward conscious living. ROOTS the Beauty Underground became her vehicle for proving that you can build something profitable while staying true to your values, something I know resonates with so many of us trying to navigate purpose-driven leadership.

What struck me most about our conversation was how Laura redefined success entirely. Instead of chasing the biggest revenue numbers or the flashiest partnerships, she's measuring impact. She's building a woman-powered brand by intentionally cultivating relationships that matter, creating products that align with her deepest values, and showing other mothers that we can create the change we want to see in the world. Her approach to leadership isn't about commanding from the top—it's about building community from the ground up.

The courage it takes to walk away from security and step into the unknown isn't just admirable—it's necessary if we want to create businesses that actually matter. Laura's journey proves that when you align your personal mission with your professional calling, you don't just build a company, you build a legacy. Her story will challenge you to examine where you might be playing it too safe and inspire you to take the leap toward something that lights you up from the inside out—plus, there's a quiz at the end that'll help you identify your own purpose-driven leadership style.

Laura Linsenmeyer is a clean beauty pioneer and founder of ROOTS the Beauty Underground, based in Laguna Beach. A former film and TV makeup artist, she transitioned from Hollywood to entrepreneurship to create a purpose-driven, non-toxic beauty brand that champions community and conscious living.

item.title: How Brooke Briggs Built Two Heart-Centered Businesses While Raising Kids
item.content:encoded:
What if your kids' development and your business success could actually fuel each other instead of competing?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I met Brooke through her incredible work creating learning experiences that honor how children actually develop, and I knew immediately I had to have her on the show. Her approach to building businesses that align with both her values and her role as a mother offers a completely different model for what's possible.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Passion and purpose can grow side by side when you align your business with your values.
  2. Building community requires invisible labor that often goes unrecognized but creates lasting impact.
  3. Sensory-rich learning and outdoor play are essential for children's development in today's digital world.
  4. You can successfully balance motherhood and business ownership when your work feels meaningful.
  5. Creating connections to nature and rhythm helps children develop authentic relationships with themselves and others.
  6. Family bonding happens naturally when parents and children engage in shared learning experiences.
  7. Purpose-driven businesses that serve community needs can thrive while making a difference.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Brooke's work is rooted in connection — to nature, to rhythm, and to the soul of childhood."

The big idea

You know that feeling when someone is doing exactly what they're meant to do? That's Brooke Briggs. She's built not one, but two thriving businesses centered around children's development—and she's done it while raising her own kids and staying true to what matters most to her. But here's what makes her story so compelling: she didn't sacrifice her business ambitions for motherhood or vice versa. Instead, she found a way to let them strengthen each other.

Brooke's journey started with music classes, but it evolved into something much bigger—a farm-based learning experience that gives children what they're actually craving: sensory-rich environments, authentic connections to nature, and the kind of rhythm that helps them develop genuine relationships with themselves and others. What struck me most about our conversation was how she talks about the invisible labor of building community. You know what I mean—all those behind-the-scenes efforts that create lasting impact but rarely get recognized or celebrated.

We dove deep into why outdoor play and hands-on learning aren't just 'nice to have' extras in today's digital world—they're essential. Brooke shared how she's watched children transform when they're given space to learn through their senses and move their bodies in natural environments. But this isn't just about the kids. It's about how purpose-driven businesses can actually thrive when they serve real community needs, and how meaningful work changes the entire dynamic of balancing motherhood and entrepreneurship.

What really resonated with me was Brooke's insight about family bonding happening naturally when parents and children engage in shared learning experiences together. She's created spaces where this happens organically, and the ripple effects are incredible. Her businesses prove that when you align your work with your values, you don't have to choose between making an impact and making money—you can absolutely do both. If you're ready to explore what heart-centered business building actually looks like in practice, you'll want to dive into the full conversation and discover which leadership approach will unlock your next level of success.

item.title: How to Build Unshakable Confidence and Rise Up Without Permission
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that confidence isn't something you're born with—it's actually a skill you can master?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think some people were just naturally confident and the rest of us had to fake it till we made it. Then I met Nada Lena Nasserdeen, and she completely shifted my perspective on what confidence really is and how we can build it intentionally.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Confidence is a learnable skill, not an innate personality trait that you either have or don't have.
  2. Modern leadership requires authenticity and the courage to bridge the gap between who you are and who you're meant to be.
  3. Overcoming imposter syndrome starts with shifting your mindset and recognizing your inherent worth and capabilities.
  4. Rising up in leadership means stopping playing small and stepping into your purpose without waiting for permission.
  5. Building unshakable confidence requires consistent practice and the willingness to embrace discomfort as part of growth.
  6. Visibility and leadership challenges are shared experiences among women that can be overcome with the right strategies.
  7. Your diverse background and experiences are assets that shape your unique vision and leadership approach.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Confidence is a skill, not a personality trait — and leadership begins when we stop playing small and start rising into our purpose."

The big idea

You know that voice in your head that whispers you're not ready, not qualified, not enough? The one that keeps you waiting for someone else to give you permission to step into your power? Here's what Nada and I discovered in this conversation: that voice is lying to you, and confidence isn't some magical trait that other women were born with while you got skipped over.

Confidence is a skill—just like learning to drive or mastering Excel. And like any skill, it gets stronger with practice. Nada breaks down exactly how to build what she calls "unshakable confidence" by shifting your mindset from seeking external validation to recognizing your inherent worth. We dive deep into why imposter syndrome hits high-achieving women so hard and how your diverse background isn't a weakness to overcome—it's actually your secret weapon that shapes your unique leadership approach.

The truth about modern leadership is that it requires authenticity and the courage to bridge the gap between who you are right now and who you're meant to become. That means stopping the small-playing, the waiting for permission, the convincing yourself you need one more certification or approval before you can step up. Nada shares the exact strategies she uses with executive clients to overcome visibility challenges and embrace discomfort as part of growth, not something to avoid.

What hit me hardest in this conversation was realizing how many of us are carrying leadership challenges that feel so personal and isolating, when they're actually shared experiences among ambitious women everywhere. The path to unshakable confidence isn't about becoming someone else—it's about consistent practice in becoming more of who you already are. If you're ready to stop waiting for permission and start building confidence as intentionally as you'd build any other professional skill, you need to dive into the full conversation and discover exactly where you are on your own confidence journey.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Nada Lena Nasserdeen

Nada Lena Nasserdeen is the Founder & CEO of Rise Up For You, author, executive coach, and former college professor. She is a global advocate for leadership, confidence, and self-worth who has helped thousands of professionals rise into their full potential from the classroom to the boardroom.

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had one of those conversations with Lindsay Buwalda that left me feeling both seen and inspired.   1. The invisible labor women carry extends far beyond household tasks into emotional and logistical work that often goes unrecognized.
  2. Being underestimated as a woman in business can actually become a strategic advantage when you learn to leverage it.
  3. Technology can be a powerful equalizer for creative entrepreneurs juggling multiple roles and responsibilities.
  4. Personal influence grows when you embrace intentional design in both business and life decisions.
  5. Creative leadership requires courage to rise above traditional expectations and forge your own path.
  6. Balancing motherhood and entrepreneurship means accepting that perfection isn't the goal—progress is.

The big idea

As a creative entrepreneur building her empire while raising her family, Lindsay's journey from creative chaos to intentional leadership offers a masterclass in leveraging what makes us different as women in business.

The invisible labor we carry goes far beyond the dishes and laundry—it's the emotional intelligence, the logistical orchestration, the ability to hold multiple moving pieces together while still showing up as visionary leaders. Lindsay breaks down how recognizing this invisible work as actual skill sets can transform how you position yourself in business. When people underestimate your strategic capabilities because you lead with creativity and heart, you're actually gathering intelligence while they're busy overlooking you.

What struck me most about our conversation was Lindsay's approach to using technology as an equalizer. You don't have to choose between being the nurturing mother and the strategic CEO—you can design systems that support both versions of yourself. She's proof that creative leadership isn't about conforming to traditional business models; it's about having the courage to forge a path that honors both your ambitions and your values.

The real game-changer? Shifting from perfectionist paralysis to progress-focused momentum. Lindsay shares how she's learned to protect her energy for what truly matters, set boundaries that actually stick, and build a sustainable business that grows alongside her family rather than despite it. Her insights on turning creative chaos into intentional design will challenge everything you think you know about balancing motherhood and entrepreneurship. item.title: Why Writing My Book Wasn't the Scary Part: A Lesson in Vulnerability
item.content:encoded:
What if the scariest part of your biggest breakthrough isn't creating it, but letting the world see it?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been sitting on something vulnerable for weeks, and today I'm peeling back the curtain on why hitting 'publish' on my book felt more terrifying than writing every raw, honest word. Sometimes the conversations we need most are the ones that make our hands shake.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Fear is often a signal that you're growing and moving in the right direction.
  2. Writing something vulnerable isn't as scary as sharing it with the world.
  3. Women hold entire worlds together before 9 AM and still question if they've done enough.
  4. Leading with vulnerability and beginner's mind creates authentic connection.
  5. Invisible labor of women deserves recognition and honor in leadership conversations.
  6. Taking the leap requires moving forward even when your hands are shaking.
  7. Perseverance through burnout and fear is part of the journey to authentic leadership.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"There are women holding entire worlds together before 9 AM, and still questioning if they've done enough."

The big idea

There's this myth that once you've written something deeply personal, the hard part is over. But here's what I learned: pouring your heart onto pages in the safety of your own space is nothing compared to the moment you hand that vulnerability over to the world. When I finished writing my book, I thought I'd conquered my fears. I was wrong. The real fear was just beginning.

You know that feeling when you've created something that matters—really matters—and suddenly you're faced with the choice to keep it safe or let it breathe in the world? That's where I found myself, hands literally shaking as I prepared to share stories about the invisible work we do as women, the leadership lessons learned in grocery store aisles, and the quiet strength it takes to hold entire worlds together before most people have their first cup of coffee. Fear showed up not as a stop sign, but as a GPS signal pointing me toward growth.

Here's what struck me most: we spend so much time questioning whether we've done enough, achieved enough, contributed enough—even after orchestrating morning routines that would make military strategists weep with envy. We manage households, careers, relationships, and still wonder if we deserve a seat at the leadership table. But what if that invisible labor, that intuitive ability to see what needs to happen and make it happen, is exactly the leadership skill the world needs more of?

Leading with vulnerability isn't about oversharing or being weak—it's about showing up authentically when your knees are knocking. It's about embracing beginner's mind even when you're running a nine-figure company. It's about recognizing that the very qualities that make you question yourself might be your greatest leadership assets. The path through burnout and fear isn't around these feelings, but straight through them, carrying your truth even when your hands are shaking.

This conversation isn't just about writing a book—it's about every moment you've had something valuable to offer but hesitated because it felt too vulnerable, too real, too much. Ready to explore what happens when you honor your own invisible labor and step fully into your power?

item.title: How One Mom Used Entrepreneurship to Reclaim Her Power and Transform Her Family
item.content:encoded:
What if becoming an entrepreneur wasn't about building a business, but about rebuilding yourself?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had one of those conversations with Kathleen Reed that left me thinking for days afterward. Here's a woman who didn't just start a business—she used entrepreneurship as a vehicle to completely transform how she shows up for her family and herself.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Entrepreneurship can be a powerful tool for reclaiming personal agency and creating the life you want on your own terms.
  2. Orofacial myofunctional therapy addresses foundational issues that impact sleep, growth, and overall family wellness.
  3. Leadership doesn't require a fancy title—it happens in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines every day.
  4. Technology and AI can be leveraged to streamline work processes and create more time for meaningful family moments.
  5. Making invisible labor visible in both home and business settings is essential for sustainable success.
  6. Faith and family values can serve as powerful guiding principles in entrepreneurial ventures.
  7. Taking time for rest and adventure, like off-grid family experiences, is crucial for maintaining perspective and joy.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership doesn't require a fancy title—it happens in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're managing everyone else's needs but somehow your own voice gets quieter and quieter? Kathleen Reed was drowning in that exact space when she made a decision that changed everything. Instead of accepting the overwhelm as her permanent reality, she turned to entrepreneurship—not as an escape from motherhood, but as a way to reclaim her power within it.

What makes Kathleen's story so compelling isn't just that she built a successful practice in orofacial myofunctional therapy (helping families address foundational health issues that impact sleep and wellness). It's how she used that journey to make all the invisible labor in her life suddenly visible and valuable. You'll hear how she leveraged technology and AI not to replace the meaningful work, but to eliminate the busy work that was stealing time from what actually mattered to her family.

This conversation will shift how you think about leadership entirely. Kathleen proves that the most powerful leadership happens in kitchens and carpool lines, not corner offices. She shares how her faith and family values became the GPS for every business decision, and why taking time for rest and adventure—like their off-grid family experiences—isn't selfish, it's strategic.

But here's what hit me hardest: Kathleen's realization that entrepreneurship isn't about building a business that takes you away from your family—it's about creating work that integrates so seamlessly with your values that it enhances everything else. If you've ever wondered whether there's a different way to think about success, career, and motherhood, this episode will give you permission to reimagine all of it and discover exactly where your own untapped leadership potential is hiding.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kathleen Reed

Kathleen Reed is the founder of West Coast Myofunctional Therapy, a specialized practice focused on orofacial myofunctional therapy for children and adults. As a mother of three and passionate advocate for whole-family wellness, she combines her expertise in health with her entrepreneurial spirit to help families achieve better sleep, growth, and vitality.

item.title: How Single Moms Can Rebuild Confidence and Find Balance After Burnout
item.content:encoded:
What if losing yourself completely was actually the first step to finding who you were meant to become?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I'll be honest—this conversation with Jessica Renee hit me right in the chest. As someone who's navigated building a business while raising six kids, I know how easy it is to lose pieces of yourself along the way. Jessica's story reminded me why rebuilding isn't just possible—it's necessary.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. It's never too late to start rebuilding your life and confidence, even after experiencing complete burnout.
  2. Single motherhood doesn't disqualify you from pursuing entrepreneurial dreams and personal growth.
  3. Fueling yourself first isn't selfish—it's essential for showing up as your best self for your family.
  4. Small, intentional daily habits can create massive shifts in confidence and overall life balance.
  5. Using tools like AI can help streamline both work and home life, giving you precious time back.
  6. Rebuilding your identity after motherhood requires patience and intentional work, but it's absolutely possible.
  7. Wellness habits and mindset shifts are foundational to creating lasting change in your life.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You can rebuild, one small step at a time—it's never too late to start again."

The big idea

You know that moment when you look in the mirror and barely recognize the woman staring back? Jessica Renee lived that reality. After experiencing complete burnout as a single mom, she found herself at rock bottom—exhausted, depleted, and questioning everything about who she'd become. But here's what I love about Jessica's story: she didn't just survive that season, she used it as fuel to completely rebuild her life from the ground up.

In our conversation, Jessica gets brutally honest about what it really takes to start over when you feel like you have nothing left to give. She breaks down how she began with the smallest possible steps—literally just five minutes of intentional movement each day—and built from there. What struck me most was her reframe around self-care: it's not about bubble baths and face masks (though those are nice), it's about the non-negotiable daily habits that keep you mentally, physically, and emotionally equipped to handle your life.

We dive deep into the practical side too. Jessica shares how she leveraged AI tools to streamline everything from meal planning to work tasks, essentially buying herself back hours each week. She talks about the mindset shifts that were game-changers—like realizing that taking care of herself first wasn't selfish, but actually the most generous thing she could do for her family. Single motherhood, she learned, didn't disqualify her from pursuing her entrepreneurial dreams; it actually sharpened her focus and made her more resourceful.

What I want you to hear in this episode is that rebuilding doesn't require perfection—it requires intention. Jessica proves that you can reclaim your confidence, rediscover your purpose, and create the balance you crave, even after hitting your absolute lowest point. The woman you were before motherhood? She's still there. And the woman you're becoming? She's even stronger than you imagine. Listen to the full episode and take our confidence-building quiz to discover which small step you should take first on your own rebuilding journey.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Jessica Renee

Jessica Renee is a solo mama, part-time hairstylist, Arbonne rep, and wellness advocate who is on a journey of rebuilding confidence and balance after experiencing burnout in the early years of motherhood. She's passionate about helping other moms reclaim their identity while balancing entrepreneurship and self-care.

item.title: How to Build Multiple Businesses While Homeschooling: Heather Gerber's Blueprint
item.content:encoded:
What if building multiple businesses while homeschooling isn't the chaos everyone thinks it is?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to bring Heather Gerber on because she's living proof that the either-or mentality we've been fed is complete garbage. When I met someone running two successful businesses while homeschooling AND creating genuine community impact, I knew you needed to hear her story.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Women can successfully run multiple businesses without having to choose one path over another.
  2. Community connection matters as much as curriculum when creating educational programs.
  3. Leading with service and heart creates more sustainable and meaningful businesses.
  4. Alternative education models can provide the flexibility modern families desperately need.
  5. Building values-driven businesses allows you to make impact while generating income.
  6. Entrepreneurship and motherhood can enhance each other rather than compete.
  7. Creating educational spaces that support both kids and parents fills a crucial market gap.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Women can build multiple businesses, nurture their families, and create meaningful community impact — all without having to choose one path over another"

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about building multiple businesses: the skills that make you an incredible mother are the exact same ones that make you an unstoppable entrepreneur. Heather Gerber discovered this truth while juggling her established tanning business and launching Guiding Our Days, all while homeschooling her children. Instead of these roles competing against each other, they created a synergy that amplified her success in every area.

What struck me most about Heather's approach is how she refused to buy into the scarcity mindset that tells us we have to choose. When she saw a gap in educational resources that truly supported both children and parents, she didn't wait for someone else to fill it. She built Guiding Our Days as a values-driven business that generates real income while creating meaningful impact. This isn't about hustle culture or burning yourself out—it's about recognizing that your heart for service can be your most profitable business strategy.

The conversation gets really practical when Heather breaks down how she structures her days and manages the mental load of multiple ventures. You'll discover why community connection matters more than perfect curriculum, and how alternative education models aren't just trendy—they're essential for families who want flexibility without sacrificing excellence. Heather proves that when you lead with your values and build businesses that align with your life, rather than fighting against it, everything becomes more sustainable.

What I love most is how Heather reframes entrepreneurship and motherhood as complementary forces rather than competing priorities. She's created educational spaces that fill a crucial market gap while building the flexible lifestyle her family needs. If you've ever wondered whether you can have both impact and income, or if you're tired of people telling you to 'just pick one thing,' this episode will give you the permission and the blueprint to build the multi-faceted life you actually want.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Heather Gerber

Heather Gerber is a serial entrepreneur, homeschooling mom, and founder of Guiding Our Days, an educational program supporting public charter independent study students. She successfully runs multiple businesses including her long-time tanning business while creating meaningful community impact through education.

item.title: Why Power Skills Are the Future of Leadership: AI-Powered Microlearning Insights
item.content:encoded:
What if the 'soft skills' you've been told to develop are actually the hardest currency in leadership?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot lately about how we've completely misnamed the skills that matter most. When Ioanna Mantzouridou and I sat down to talk about AI-powered microlearning, what struck me wasn't the technology—it was her insight that empathy, intuition, and emotional intelligence aren't nice-to-haves anymore. They're the difference between leaders who survive and leaders who scale.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Power skills like empathy, communication, and emotional intelligence are becoming more critical than technical skills in leadership roles.
  2. Microlearning allows busy professionals to develop essential skills in just 5-minute daily sessions, making growth sustainable and manageable.
  3. AI can effectively teach human skills by providing personalized feedback and adaptive learning experiences tailored to individual needs.
  4. Systems thinking and intuition are essential leadership capabilities that women often excel at naturally but need to recognize and develop intentionally.
  5. Scaling human connection in digital environments requires intentional strategies and tools that prioritize authentic relationship building.
  6. The invisible leadership work that women do daily—emotional labor, relationship maintenance, and team cohesion—deserves recognition and strategic development.
  7. Building confidence at work is a learnable skill that can be developed through consistent practice and the right learning frameworks.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The key to leadership isn't just strategy and hustle — but empathy, confidence, and connection."

The big idea

You know that feeling when someone finally puts words to something you've always known but couldn't quite articulate? That's exactly what happened when Ioanna shared her perspective on what she calls 'power skills'—the human capabilities that are becoming more valuable than any technical expertise as AI reshapes our world. While everyone's scrambling to learn the latest tech stack, the real competitive advantage lies in the skills many of us have been developing all along without even realizing it.

Here's what caught my attention: Ioanna's research shows that the invisible work we do every day—reading the room, managing team dynamics, building genuine connections—these aren't just parts of our job. They're the job. But here's the kicker: most of us are doing this work intuitively without systematically developing these skills or getting credit for them. We're leaving money and influence on the table because we haven't learned to recognize and articulate our natural strengths.

The conversation took a fascinating turn when we dove into how AI is actually making human skills more essential, not less. Ioanna's company has cracked the code on teaching empathy and emotional intelligence through technology, using microlearning sessions that fit into the craziest schedules. We're talking five-minute daily bursts that can fundamentally shift how you show up as a leader. The science behind it is incredible—AI provides personalized feedback and adapts to your learning style in ways that traditional training never could.

What I loved most was Ioanna's take on systems thinking and intuition as leadership superpowers that women often possess naturally but need to develop intentionally. She shared specific strategies for scaling authentic connection in our increasingly digital world, and practical frameworks for building confidence that goes beyond fake-it-till-you-make-it mindset tricks. This isn't about adding more to your plate—it's about recognizing the value of what you're already bringing and amplifying it strategically. If you've ever wondered whether your natural way of leading actually gives you an edge, this episode will give you the roadmap to find out exactly where you stand and how to leverage it.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Ioanna Mantzouridou

Ioanna Mantzouridou is the founder of Dextego, a groundbreaking platform that uses AI-powered microlearning to help sales teams develop power skills like communication, negotiation, and emotional intelligence. She brings extensive global leadership experience to her entrepreneurial journey, focusing on reshaping the future of learning through innovative technology solutions.

item.title: From Invisible to Unstoppable: How Women Leaders Can Transform Burnout into Purpose
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you're doing behind the scenes isn't invisible labor—it's world-shaping leadership?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to get real with myself about something that's been eating at me. For too long, I've watched brilliant women—myself included—dismiss the incredible leadership work we do every single day as 'just what needs to get done.' This conversation is me finally saying what we all need to hear.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Invisible labor performed by women is actually a form of world-shaping leadership that deserves recognition and honor.
  2. Burnout is not a personal failure but a cultural symptom of systems that don't value women's contributions.
  3. You can reclaim your purpose through small one-degree shifts rather than dramatic life overhauls.
  4. Domestic diplomacy, ritual creation, and sisterhood are revolutionary acts of leadership.
  5. You don't need credentials or permission to lead - you already have the spark within you.
  6. Presence matters more than five-year plans when it comes to creating meaningful change.
  7. Every woman has the power to turn pain into purpose and become a changemaker right where she is.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't need credentials to lead. You need love."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're managing seventeen moving pieces at once—coordinating schedules, solving problems, anticipating needs, keeping everyone happy—and then someone asks what you do all day? That invisible work you're doing? It's not invisible. It's revolutionary leadership, and it's time we started treating it that way.

Here's what I've learned after years of building companies and raising six kids: burnout isn't your personal failure. It's a cultural symptom of systems that refuse to value what women bring to the table. When you're exhausted from holding everything together, that's not weakness—that's you carrying leadership load that would break most people. The skills you use for domestic diplomacy, the way you create rituals that hold your family together, the sisterhood you build with other women? These aren't side projects. They're revolutionary acts of leadership that literally shape the world.

But here's where it gets interesting: you don't need to blow up your life to reclaim your power. I'm talking about one-degree shifts, not dramatic overhauls. Small changes that honor the leader you already are while creating space for the leader you're becoming. Because the truth is, you don't need more credentials or anyone's permission to lead. You already have everything you need—that spark has been there all along.

The game-changer isn't perfecting some five-year plan. It's showing up present to this moment, to the power you already hold, to the ways you're already changing everything around you. Every woman has this ability to transform pain into purpose, to become a changemaker right where she stands. The question isn't whether you're leadership material—you already are. The question is: are you ready to see it? Ready to own it? Ready to discover exactly what kind of unstoppable leader you really are when you stop making your power invisible?
