item.title: From Corporate Law to Fighting Human Trafficking: One Woman's Bold Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What if the corporate skills you've mastered could save children from trafficking rings tomorrow?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've met a lot of brave women in my career, but Leigh Dundas operates on a different level entirely. Her willingness to leave behind a lucrative corporate law practice to literally go undercover and rescue trafficked children stopped me in my tracks. This conversation challenged everything I thought I knew about what it means to lead with purpose.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. True leadership often requires making bold career pivots that align with your deeper values and purpose.
  2. Protecting human rights and freedoms requires both grassroots action and individual courage to stand up for what's right.
  3. The emotional toll of advocacy work demands intentional self-care strategies and strong support systems.
  4. Corporate skills can be powerfully redirected toward social justice and humanitarian causes.
  5. Medical freedom and civil liberties are fundamental human rights worth defending at every level.
  6. Undercover operations against trafficking require meticulous planning, courage, and collaboration with local authorities.
  7. Grassroots movements have the power to create meaningful change when individuals step up to lead.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership isn't about titles or corner offices—it's about having the courage to fight for what's right, even when the stakes are high."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about making a massive career pivot: the skills that made you successful in one arena can become your secret weapon in another. Leigh Dundas discovered this when she walked away from high-stakes corporate litigation to fight human trafficking—and her legal expertise became the foundation for some of the most dangerous and impactful work imaginable.

During our conversation, Leigh revealed how the meticulous planning skills she'd honed in corporate boardrooms translated directly to coordinating undercover operations with local authorities. The attention to detail that once served Fortune 500 clients now helps her navigate life-or-death situations where one mistake could compromise an entire rescue mission. It's a powerful reminder that the capabilities you're building right now—whether you're managing teams, negotiating deals, or solving complex problems—have applications you haven't even considered yet.

But here's what really struck me: Leigh's journey isn't just about career transformation. It's about what happens when you align your professional skills with your deepest values, even when that alignment demands everything from you. She spoke candidly about the emotional toll of advocacy work and the non-negotiable importance of building support systems that can sustain you through the hardest moments. This isn't the sanitized version of 'follow your passion'—this is the raw truth about what it costs to lead with conviction.

What fascinated me most was how Leigh's work extends beyond individual rescue operations into the broader fight for medical freedom and civil liberties. She's living proof that grassroots movements powered by individual courage can create systemic change. Her approach to leadership doesn't wait for permission or consensus—it identifies what needs to be done and finds a way to do it, regardless of the personal cost.

This conversation will challenge you to think differently about the intersection of professional skills and personal purpose, and Leigh's insights about maintaining your humanity while fighting for others will stay with you long after you finish listening—make sure you read the full breakdown and take our leadership assessment to discover how your current skills could serve a bigger mission.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Leigh Dundas

Leigh Dundas is a human rights attorney, modern-day abolitionist, and award-winning public speaker who transitioned from representing Fortune 500 companies to leading undercover missions against child trafficking networks. She's become a powerful voice for civil liberties and medical freedom, advocating for human rights both globally and domestically.

item.title: From High School Coach to Tech CEO: How to Build a Company by Accident
item.content:encoded:
What if the most successful businesses aren't built in boardrooms, but by women solving problems with heart and grit?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to bring Alyssa Nolte on because her story completely flips the script on how we think about building companies. She went from volunteering as a high school dance coach to running a tech company—not through some grand master plan, but by solving real problems she actually lived through. Her journey proves that your most 'random' experiences might be your greatest leadership training ground.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Leadership skills can be developed in unexpected places, including volunteer coaching roles.
  2. The most successful businesses often solve problems you've personally experienced.
  3. Systems thinking and intuition are not opposites but complementary leadership tools.
  4. Invisible labor in motherhood translates directly to valuable leadership capabilities.
  5. Starting before you feel ready is often the key to breakthrough success.
  6. Humor and authenticity can be powerful tools for leading through uncertainty.
  7. Building a company accidentally can lead to more authentic and sustainable growth.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Invisible labor IS leadership and women need to own that truth"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're managing seventeen moving pieces at once—kids' schedules, household systems, volunteer commitments—and someone asks what you 'do'? Alyssa's story will change how you answer that question forever. She built a tech company not because she had an MBA or a brilliant business plan, but because she saw problems everywhere and couldn't help but solve them.

What started as volunteer coaching turned into recognizing patterns, building systems, and leading teams through uncertainty. Sound familiar? That's because the invisible labor you do every day—the project management, the emotional intelligence, the ability to pivot when everything falls apart—these aren't just survival skills. They're CEO skills. Alyssa proves that your intuition isn't the opposite of strategic thinking; it's fuel for it.

The most powerful part of our conversation wasn't about funding rounds or growth metrics. It was about how she started before she felt ready, used humor to navigate the unknown, and trusted that solving real problems would lead somewhere meaningful. She didn't accidentally stumble into success—she accidentally discovered that her authentic approach to leadership was exactly what her company needed to grow sustainably.

Here's what hit me hardest: the businesses that feel most authentic often come from the most unexpected places. When you stop trying to fit someone else's blueprint for success and start building from your actual experiences and instincts, magic happens. Ready to see how your own 'random' experiences might be preparing you for something bigger than you imagined?

About Alyssa Nolte

Alyssa Nolte is the CEO of TruVue and host of two podcasts: The Growth Signal and Taking Back Monday. She's an accidental entrepreneur who built her tech company from the ground up, starting as a volunteer dance coach and evolving into a systems-thinking leader who believes intuition and strategy can coexist in business.

item.title: From Corporate Careers to Restroom Revolution: The UNICORN Story
item.description: Episode 26 | with Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan
item.content:encoded:
What if the most overlooked spaces in every building hold the key to your next breakthrough business idea?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've built companies by solving problems others don't see, but this conversation with Denielle and Thyme opened my eyes to gaps hiding in plain sight. Their story proves that the biggest opportunities aren't always the most obvious ones — sometimes they're behind doors we walk past every day.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Leadership often starts with noticing what others overlook rather than inventing something completely new.
  2. Building a business with family requires clear boundaries and defined roles to maintain both relationships and success.
  3. Mission-driven companies can scale to Fortune 500 clients when they solve real problems with thoughtful solutions.
  4. Restroom equity is about dignity, wellness, and inclusion, not just convenience.
  5. Transforming invisible gaps into business opportunities requires courage to challenge the status quo.
  6. Co-CEO partnerships can work when both leaders bring complementary strengths and shared vision.
  7. Scaling a values-based company requires maintaining your core mission while adapting to larger market demands.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership isn't always about inventing something new — sometimes it's about noticing what's been ignored, and daring to redesign it."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you walk into a public restroom and immediately start calculating the fastest exit strategy? Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan felt it too, but instead of just grimacing and moving on, these cousins saw a massive business opportunity. What started as a shared frustration with inadequate restroom facilities became UNICORN — a company that's now revolutionizing restroom equity for Fortune 500 clients across the country.

Here's what struck me most about their journey: they didn't invent anything groundbreaking. They simply noticed what the rest of us have been trained to ignore. Think about it — how many times have you encountered a restroom that lacked basic necessities, felt unsafe, or seemed designed without women in mind? Denielle and Thyme turned that universal frustration into a mission-driven business that's changing how companies think about employee dignity and inclusion. Their approach wasn't just about stocking better products; it was about recognizing that restroom access is fundamentally about human dignity, workplace wellness, and creating spaces where everyone can show up fully.

What I love about their story is how they navigated building a business as family members without destroying their relationship. They'll walk you through the hard conversations about boundaries, the strategic decision to become co-CEOs, and how they leveraged their complementary strengths instead of competing with each other. It's a masterclass in partnership — whether you're considering working with family or just trying to understand how successful co-leadership actually functions in practice.

But perhaps most importantly, Denielle and Thyme prove that mission-driven companies can scale without losing their soul. They've managed to maintain their core values while adapting to serve massive corporate clients, showing you exactly how to build a business that matters without sacrificing profitability. Their journey from identifying an invisible problem to creating systemic change will challenge how you think about opportunity recognition and give you permission to trust your instincts about the gaps you notice in your own world. Ready to discover how two women turned an everyday frustration into a movement that's reshaping workplace equity one restroom at a time?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan

Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan are cousins and co-founders of UNICORN, a company revolutionizing restroom equity through innovative product dispensers. They left successful corporate careers to build a mission-driven business now serving Fortune 500 companies including Chase, American Express, and major airports across the country.

item.title: How to Build an Ethical Fashion Empire Without Burning Out
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest career burnout was actually the blueprint for building a business that serves your values?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about how we can build businesses that don't require us to sacrifice our souls in the process. When I sat down with Kathryn Letson, founder of By The Sea Company, I knew I was talking to someone who had cracked the code on creating something meaningful without the typical entrepreneurial burnout we've all been told is 'just part of the journey.'

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Burnout can become the catalyst for creating a business model that truly fits your life and values.
  2. Ethical fashion means considering the entire supply chain, from materials to manufacturing to worker conditions.
  3. You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something significant in business.
  4. Starting small at farmers markets can be a powerful way to test and refine your business concept.
  5. Living in alignment with your environment can significantly impact your creativity and leadership style.
  6. Building a sustainable business requires designing systems that support both profit and principles.
  7. The future of fashion lies in innovative, eco-conscious textiles that don't compromise on style or function.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something big."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about burnout: it's often your internal compass screaming that you're headed in the wrong direction. Kathryn Letson learned this the hard way during her corporate fashion career, but instead of pushing through like so many of us do, she listened. What came next wasn't just a career pivot—it was a complete reimagining of how business could work when you refuse to compromise your values or your wellbeing.

Building By The Sea Company into an ethical fashion empire with over 3,000 retailers didn't happen through 80-hour weeks or 'fake it till you make it' mentality. Instead, Kathryn started small, testing her concept at farmers markets and building slowly with intention. She discovered that ethical fashion isn't just a marketing buzzword—it's a commitment to examining every single piece of your supply chain, from the materials you source to the conditions your workers experience. This level of intentionality created something remarkable: a business model that actually supports the life she wants to live.

What struck me most about our conversation was how Kathryn talks about alignment—not just with your values, but with your environment and your natural rhythms. She's proof that you can build something significant without sacrificing your sanity, and that sustainable business practices extend beyond your products to how you structure your days, your team, and your growth strategy.

The future of fashion is changing rapidly, and innovative leaders like Kathryn are showing us that eco-conscious doesn't have to mean compromising on style or profitability. Her approach to building systems that support both principles and profit is something every woman leader needs to hear, regardless of your industry. There's so much more to unpack from this conversation about turning burnout into breakthrough, and I've created something special to help you apply these insights to your own leadership journey.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kathryn Letson

Kathryn Letson is the founder of By The Sea Company, an ethical fashion brand that has grown to serve over 3,000 retailers. After experiencing burnout in NYC's high-pressure fashion industry, she pivoted to create a sustainable business model rooted in ergonomic design and eco-conscious textiles from her base in Kauai.

item.title: Why Men Need to Step Up Emotionally at Home (Not Just Financially)
item.content:encoded:
What if your husband's biggest contribution to your family isn't his paycheck—but his emotional presence?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been waiting for the right man to join me on this show, and Bart Morse was absolutely worth the wait. As someone who's navigated the complexities of building a career while raising six kids, I know firsthand how crucial it is for partners to step up beyond just financial provision—and Bart's framework for true masculine leadership at home completely shifted how I think about partnership.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. True masculine leadership at home goes far beyond providing financially—it requires emotional presence and shared responsibility for family systems.
  2. The 'nice guy' syndrome and people-pleasing behaviors actually create distance in relationships rather than the safety they're intended to provide.
  3. Boundaries are external limits while principles are internal values—understanding this distinction transforms how couples create safety together.
  4. Men can begin sharing the mental load by taking ownership of family calendars, systems, and forward-thinking planning.
  5. The '8 Mountains' framework provides a holistic approach to fulfillment that encompasses marriage, parenting, health, finances, and personal growth.
  6. Power struggles in marriage transform into partnership when both people commit to radical ownership of their roles and responsibilities.
  7. Shame is often the root barrier preventing men from stepping into deeper emotional leadership and authentic vulnerability.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"True leadership at home isn't just about providing and protecting—it's about emotionally showing up for your family every single day."

The big idea

Here's the truth we don't talk about enough: the mental load crisis destroying marriages isn't actually a women's issue. It's a partnership issue. And until men understand that true leadership at home requires far more than a paycheck, we'll keep having the same exhausting conversations about who remembered to schedule the dentist appointments and why the grocery list lives permanently in your brain.

Bart Morse brought a perspective I'd never heard articulated quite this way—that many men are stuck in what he calls the 'nice guy' syndrome, thinking that being agreeable and staying out of the way creates safety in relationships. But here's what's actually happening: when your partner consistently defers decisions to you, avoids conflict, and operates from people-pleasing behaviors, it creates more distance, not more connection. You end up feeling like you're managing another child instead of partnering with an equal.

The game-changer in our conversation was Bart's distinction between boundaries and principles. Boundaries are those external limits we set with others, but principles are the internal values that guide how we show up. When both partners get clear on their principles and take radical ownership of their roles—not just the easy stuff, but the forward-thinking, calendar-managing, system-creating responsibilities—that's when power struggles transform into actual partnership.

What I loved most about Bart's '8 Mountains' framework is how it gives couples a holistic roadmap for building fulfillment together across marriage, parenting, health, finances, and personal growth. It's not about perfect balance—it's about intentional investment in what matters most. And here's what might surprise you: shame is often the biggest barrier preventing the men in our lives from stepping into deeper emotional leadership. When we understand this, we can create space for the kind of vulnerability that actually strengthens relationships instead of just talking about communication in endless circles.

This conversation will challenge how you think about partnership, emotional labor, and what it really means to build a life together where both people are fully engaged. If you're ready to move beyond surface-level solutions and dive into the deeper work of creating true partnership at home, you need to read the full breakdown of Bart's framework and take our partnership assessment to see where you and your partner stand.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Bart Morse

Bart Morse is the founder of the Lion's Den Program, a transformational coaching program for men. He specializes in helping men move beyond traditional masculine roles of providing and protecting to become true emotional leaders in their relationships and families.

item.title: How to Parent with Purpose: Building Meaning and Resilience in Your Children
item.content:encoded:
What if protecting your kids from every struggle is actually setting them up to fail?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think good parenting meant smoothing every bump in the road for my six kids. Then I had this conversation with Pam Roy that completely flipped my perspective on resilience, meaning, and what our children actually need from us to thrive.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Parenting from the passenger seat means guiding your children without controlling their journey, allowing them to develop their own sense of purpose and direction.
  2. Meaning is more essential than happiness for building resilience and mental health in children.
  3. Discomfort and challenges are necessary for building resilience—protecting children from all difficulties actually weakens them.
  4. True belonging comes from authentic connection and shared values, not from fitting in or conforming to others' expectations.
  5. Teaching children to find meaning in their struggles helps them develop the mental tools to handle life's inevitable challenges.
  6. Community-based learning and mentorship provide more valuable real-world experience than traditional classroom settings alone.
  7. Traditions and rituals create powerful anchors of meaning and connection that help combat the loneliness epidemic affecting young people.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Raising resilient, purpose-driven kids begins with our own courage to live authentically."

The big idea

Here's what no parenting book will tell you: your job isn't to make your children happy. Your job is to help them find meaning—even in the hard stuff. Pam Roy brought Viktor Frankl's profound wisdom into our conversation about raising resilient kids, and honestly, it changed how I think about everything from bedtime battles to big life decisions.

The concept that hit me hardest? Parenting from the passenger seat. You're still in the car, you're still guiding the journey, but you're not white-knuckling the steering wheel trying to control every turn. Your children need to develop their own sense of direction, their own purpose. When we constantly rescue them from discomfort or failure, we're actually robbing them of the very experiences that build resilience. Think about it—every time you swoop in to fix their friendship drama or call the teacher about a missed assignment, you're sending the message that they can't handle their own lives.

Pam shared something that made me pause: meaning is more essential than happiness for mental health. We've created this culture where we're chasing happiness for our kids instead of helping them find purpose in their struggles. The children who learn to extract meaning from challenges—who understand that difficult experiences can teach them something valuable—these are the kids who thrive when life gets hard. And life will get hard.

What really struck me was her insight about belonging versus fitting in. True belonging comes from authentic connection and shared values, not from conforming to what everyone else expects. This is where family traditions and rituals become powerful anchors. They create meaning and connection that combat the loneliness epidemic we're seeing in young people. It's not about elaborate celebrations—it's about consistent, meaningful practices that say 'this is who we are, this is what matters to us.'

The conversation also dove deep into community-based learning and how real-world mentorship often provides more valuable experience than traditional classroom settings alone. As leaders and mothers, we have the opportunity to create environments where our children can practice resilience, discover their own meaning, and build authentic connections. Ready to discover your parenting approach and see how you can guide your children toward greater resilience and purpose?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Pam Roy

Pam Roy is a writer, speaker, and visionary thought leader who reimagines parenting, education, and purpose in our rapidly changing world. She is the author of Parenting from the Passenger Seat and Viktor E. Frankl: The Search for Meaning, a partner at the Viktor Frankl Institute of America, and an executive producer of films spotlighting the youth mental health crisis.

item.title: Why Self-Management is the Foundation of Great Leadership with Lynda Harvey
item.content:encoded:
The most effective leaders I know aren't the ones with fancy titles—they're the ones who've mastered managing themselves first.

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking about this conversation with Lynda Harvey for weeks now. She said something that stopped me cold: knowing who you don't want to be as a leader is just as powerful as knowing who you do want to become. As someone who's navigated both motherhood and C-suite leadership, that hit different.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Self-management is the foundational skill that must be mastered before you can effectively manage others.
  2. Mothers naturally develop many management skills through daily parenting that translate directly to workplace leadership.
  3. Knowing who you don't want to be as a leader is just as important as defining who you want to become.
  4. Tactical missions and small daily practices build leadership confidence more effectively than waiting for formal titles.
  5. Emotional intelligence and practical empathy are becoming essential leadership skills in the modern workplace.
  6. Building a personal brand around your expertise can create new career opportunities beyond traditional corporate paths.
  7. Self-awareness about your triggers and reactions allows you to lead from a place of calm rather than chaos.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership doesn't start in the boardroom—it starts in your own living room."

The big idea

Here's what no one tells you about leadership: the real work happens inside you first. Lynda Harvey, former corporate executive turned leadership expert, dropped truth bombs in our conversation that every woman needs to hear. The biggest? You're already developing management skills every single day—you just don't recognize them yet.

Think about it. When you're juggling three kids' schedules, managing household logistics, and keeping everyone fed and functioning, you're practicing resource allocation, crisis management, and emotional intelligence at an elite level. But somehow, we've been trained to think these skills don't 'count' in the business world. Lynda's here to tell us that's complete nonsense. Those invisible skills you've been honing? They're your leadership superpowers waiting to be unleashed.

The conversation gets really interesting when we dig into self-management as the foundation of everything else. You can't lead a team effectively if you're still reacting from your triggers instead of responding from your center. Lynda walks us through how to build that self-awareness—not through years of therapy (though therapy is great), but through practical daily practices that build your leadership muscle one decision at a time.

What I love most about Lynda's approach is how she reframes those 'tactical missions'—small, daily leadership actions you can take right now, with or without a formal title. Because here's the thing: leadership isn't something that happens to you when someone finally recognizes your worth. It's something you step into, one conscious choice at a time.

This episode is packed with the kind of practical wisdom that makes you want to immediately text your best friend and share the insights—but first, you'll want to read the full breakdown and take our leadership readiness quiz to see where you're already winning and where you can level up next.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Lynda Harvey

Lynda Harvey is a former corporate leader at Verizon and Nordstrom who has transformed into a viral TikTok educator and entrepreneur. She now runs a thriving micro-learning platform focused on social leadership while raising two teenagers and helping other leaders develop self-awareness and emotional intelligence.

item.title: Why Leadership Is a Behavior (Not a Title) + Credit Confidence for Women
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that most women are leading brilliantly but don't have the title or paycheck to show for it?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had one of those conversations with Elizabeth Taylor that left me taking notes furiously. As someone who's built companies and raised six kids, I thought I understood leadership—but Elizabeth's take on why it's purely behavioral (not hierarchical) completely reframed how I think about showing up. Plus, her insights on credit confidence? Game-changing.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Leadership is fundamentally a behavior and mindset, not a position or title you hold.
  2. Women managers between 35-45 experience the highest rates of burnout and need specific strategies to combat this epidemic.
  3. Developing a clear leadership brand helps you show up consistently and confidently in your role.
  4. Credit confidence is a crucial but underrated leadership skill that directly impacts your freedom and opportunities.
  5. Being risk-aware rather than risk-averse allows women to make informed financial decisions with confidence.
  6. The current generational wealth transfer presents unprecedented opportunities for women who are financially prepared.
  7. Balancing ambition with rest and joy is essential for sustainable leadership success.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership isn't a title — it's a behavior."

The big idea

You're already leading—whether you realize it or not. In my conversation with Elizabeth Taylor, we unpacked why leadership has absolutely nothing to do with your business card and everything to do with how you show up every single day. If you're waiting for someone to anoint you with a fancy title before you start leading, you're missing the point entirely. Leadership is a behavior, a mindset, a way of moving through the world that creates impact regardless of your position on an org chart.

But here's what's keeping so many of us stuck: we're burned out beyond belief. Elizabeth shared some sobering statistics about women managers, particularly those of us between 35-45, experiencing the highest rates of burnout across all demographics. We're caught in this impossible middle—managing up, managing down, managing our households, managing everyone else's expectations. The solution isn't to work harder or find better time management apps. It's about developing a clear leadership brand that helps you show up consistently and confidently, without depleting yourself in the process.

Then we dove into something most leadership conversations completely ignore: your relationship with money and credit. Elizabeth calls it 'credit confidence,' and honestly, it should be required curriculum for every woman who wants real freedom and options. We're living through the largest generational wealth transfer in history, but if you're not financially prepared—if you don't understand how credit works, how to be risk-aware instead of risk-averse—you'll miss these unprecedented opportunities completely.

What struck me most about our conversation was Elizabeth's emphasis on sustainable leadership. You can't lead effectively if you're running on empty, which means building rest and joy into your leadership equation isn't optional—it's strategic. She gave me a completely new framework for thinking about ambition that doesn't require sacrificing everything else that matters. Ready to discover your leadership style and take control of your financial future? Dive into the full conversation and don't miss the assessment that'll help you identify exactly where to focus your energy next.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Elizabeth Taylor

Elizabeth Taylor, known as @thecorporatebestie, is a leadership trainer, sales coach, and organizational development expert who specializes in helping high-performing women transition into management roles without burning out. She combines her expertise in leadership development with her background in the credit industry to help women build both professional confidence and financial literacy.

item.title: From Homeschooling to Executive Coaching: Building Leaders at Home and Work
item.content:encoded:
What if raising kids on commitments instead of rules could revolutionize how you lead at work?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've always believed our parenting shapes our leadership, but Amanda Jaggard took this to a whole new level. Her approach to creating intentional culture at home—and how it transformed her business leadership—had me taking notes. This conversation will shift how you think about influence in every area of your life.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Commitments create stronger foundations than rules because they require personal ownership and buy-in from everyone involved.
  2. Creating intentional culture at home through language, agreements, and respect directly translates to better leadership skills in business.
  3. The transition from homeschooling to entrepreneurship requires recognizing that your parenting experience has developed valuable business skills.
  4. Building a coaching practice successfully requires both mastering the art of coaching and developing strong business fundamentals.
  5. Preparing for empty nest season while your children are still home allows for a smoother transition and stronger family relationships.
  6. Single mothers can raise confident leaders by modeling resilience and teaching children to take ownership of their choices.
  7. Executive coaching principles applied to parenting create households where everyone feels respected and empowered to grow.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"We raised our boys on commitments instead of rules, and it changed everything about how our family operated."

The big idea

Amanda Jaggard's journey from homeschooling mother to successful executive coach proves that the skills you develop at home are your secret leadership superpowers. In this conversation, she reveals how she built a thriving coaching practice by recognizing that everything she learned while raising her sons—from conflict resolution to creating buy-in—directly translated into business gold.

The game-changer? Amanda doesn't run her household on rules. She builds it on commitments. When everyone in the family has to personally own and agree to the standards, you create a culture of respect and accountability that most corporate teams would kill for. This isn't about being the 'cool mom'—it's about raising leaders who understand that their choices have consequences and their voice matters in shaping the family culture.

As a single mother, Amanda had to model the resilience and ownership she wanted to see in her children. She shares how preparing for empty nest season while your kids are still home creates smoother transitions and stronger relationships. Instead of dreading what comes next, you're building toward it together. Her approach to executive coaching applies the same principles: creating environments where people feel empowered to grow rather than micromanaged into compliance.

What struck me most was how Amanda recognized her parenting experience as valuable business training rather than a career gap. The transition from homeschooling to entrepreneurship required her to own the leadership skills she'd been developing all along—skills like creating psychological safety, managing different personalities, and building systems that work for everyone involved.

This conversation will challenge how you think about authority, influence, and culture-building whether you're leading a team of two or two hundred. Ready to discover how your home leadership translates to business success and take our leadership style assessment?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Amanda Jaggard

Amanda Jaggard is an Executive Coach at Novus Global and co-founder of the Meta Performance Institute. After earning her Master's in Leadership and Ministry and homeschooling her sons for nearly a decade, she transitioned to executive coaching in 2014. As a single mother, she has successfully raised leaders while building a thriving coaching practice that helps others develop both their coaching skills and business acumen.

item.title: From Corporate Executive to Entrepreneur: Lyndsay Dowd's Guide to Reinvention
item.content:encoded:
What if the job security you're desperately protecting is actually the biggest threat to your career?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to bring Lyndsay Dowd on because her story shattered everything I thought I knew about 'safe' career moves. After 23 years climbing the IBM ladder, she walked away to build something entirely her own—and her reasoning will make you rethink your entire professional strategy.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Career security is more valuable than job security in today's rapidly changing workplace landscape.
  2. Personal branding is a leadership skill, not a luxury, and essential for professional growth at any level.
  3. Transforming company culture from the inside out requires courage and strategic action.
  4. Community and genuine connections are the real fuel for both happiness and professional success.
  5. Reinvention can happen at any age or career stage with the right mindset and support system.
  6. Building an irresistible workplace starts with ditching toxic leadership patterns and leading with heart.
  7. The transition from corporate executive to entrepreneur requires embracing uncertainty while leveraging existing skills.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The secret to lasting success isn't job security — it's career security"

The big idea

You've been told to keep your head down, do good work, and climb the ladder. But what if that ladder is leaning against the wrong wall? Lyndsay Dowd spent over two decades building what looked like the perfect corporate career at IBM—until she realized that job security was actually making her less secure, not more. Her transition from corporate executive to entrepreneur isn't just inspiring; it's a masterclass in strategic career reinvention that every woman needs to hear.

Here's what Lyndsay discovered that changed everything: in today's rapidly shifting workplace, betting everything on one company is the riskiest move you can make. Instead, she started building what she calls 'career security'—a portfolio of skills, relationships, and personal brand equity that travels with you wherever you go. This isn't about being disloyal to your current role; it's about being smart enough to future-proof yourself in a world where even the most stable companies can pivot overnight.

The conversation gets especially powerful when Lyndsay breaks down personal branding—not as some superficial LinkedIn exercise, but as a genuine leadership skill that's absolutely essential for your professional survival. She shares exactly how she transformed toxic workplace cultures from the inside out, built authentic professional communities that actually move the needle, and developed the courage to bet on herself when the safe path no longer served her growth.

What I love most about Lyndsay's approach is how she proves that reinvention isn't just for twenty-somethings or people in crisis—it's for ambitious women who refuse to settle for 'good enough' at any stage of their careers. Her insights on embracing uncertainty while leveraging your existing expertise will give you a completely new framework for thinking about professional growth. Whether you're feeling stuck in your current role or dreaming of your own entrepreneurial leap, this episode will show you exactly how to build the irresistible career and workplace you actually want. item.title: How Faith and Financial Freedom Create Purpose-Driven Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What if retiring at 28 wasn't about having millions, but about rewriting your entire relationship with money?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, Hey friend, I'm still processing my conversation with Spring Eatmon, who literally retired herself at 28—not because she had it all figured out, but because she chose to align her financial decisions with her purpose instead of her paycheck. Her story will challenge everything you think you know about wealth, leadership, and what it means to build a legacy.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Financial freedom isn't about the amount of money you have, but about your relationship with money and alignment with purpose.
  2. Walking in purpose over paycheck requires courage, but creates authentic prosperity and fulfillment.
  3. Partnership in marriage and business amplifies impact when both people are aligned with their calling.
  4. Your wealth begins with understanding your worth, not the other way around.
  5. Building generational wealth requires intentional planning and legacy-focused thinking.
  6. Women must rewrite their relationship with money to step into their full leadership potential.
  7. Faith-based decision making can lead to greater financial and personal freedom than traditional career paths.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Your wealth begins with your worth and your legacy begins today"

The big idea

Spring Eatmon's story isn't your typical "quit your job and follow your dreams" narrative. When she made the bold decision to retire at 28, she wasn't sitting on a mountain of cash—she was sitting on something far more valuable: a completely transformed relationship with money and an unshakeable faith in her purpose. What she's built since then will make you question every assumption you've ever had about financial freedom and what it really takes to lead with authentic power.

Here's what hit me hardest in our conversation: Spring didn't wait until she felt "ready" or had every financial detail mapped out. She made faith-based decisions that flew in the face of conventional wisdom, and those decisions didn't just create financial freedom—they created the kind of purposeful life that most of us only dream about. She's proof that when you align your money with your mission, everything changes. Your wealth truly does begin with understanding your worth, not the other way around.

But Spring isn't just talking theory here. She's building generational wealth through intentional, legacy-focused thinking while running a purpose-driven business empire alongside her husband. Their partnership amplifies both of their impacts in ways that will inspire you to look at your own relationships—business and personal—through a completely different lens. She's rewriting the rules about what it means to be a woman in leadership, and she's doing it with the kind of bold confidence that only comes from knowing you're walking in your true calling.

This conversation will challenge you to examine your own relationship with money, purpose, and the courage it takes to choose fulfillment over a traditional paycheck. Spring's insights about faith-based decision making and authentic prosperity aren't just inspiring—they're actionable. If you've ever felt torn between playing it safe and stepping into the leadership role you know you're meant for, this episode will give you the permission and the roadmap you've been waiting for.

item.title: Why Your Mitochondria Are the Secret to Unstoppable Energy and Longevity
item.content:encoded:
What if your cells' operating system is the real reason you're exhausted by 3 PM?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think my afternoon energy crashes were just part of being a busy CEO and mom of six. Then I learned about mitochondria—not just as cellular powerhouses, but as the actual operating system controlling everything from my hormones to how I age. This conversation with Kristina Kristen completely shifted how I think about managing my energy.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Mitochondria function as your body's operating system, not just powerhouses, controlling energy, hormones, and aging processes.
  2. The NESTS Framework—Nutrition, Exercise, Sleep, Toxin avoidance, and Stress control—provides a systematic approach to mitochondrial health.
  3. Maternal mitochondrial DNA creates a unique health legacy that passes from mothers to children.
  4. Women can apply the same strategic thinking they use in business to optimize their cellular health and energy levels.
  5. Simple daily habits can dramatically improve mitochondrial function, leading to better fat burning, mental clarity, and longevity.
  6. Toxin avoidance and stress management are equally important as nutrition and exercise for cellular health.
  7. Resetting mitochondria requires a 28-day commitment but delivers lasting improvements in energy and vitality.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Your mitochondria are your body's operating system—not just the powerhouses"

The big idea

You know that feeling when your laptop starts running slow, freezing up, and you realize you need to restart the whole system? That's essentially what's happening in your body when your mitochondria aren't functioning optimally. These aren't just the 'powerhouses' of your cells—they're running your entire biological operating system, controlling everything from your energy levels to your hormones to how fast you're aging.

What fascinated me most about my conversation with Kristina is how she breaks down mitochondrial health using the NESTS Framework: Nutrition, Exercise, Sleep, Toxin avoidance, and Stress control. It's like having a systematic approach to debugging your body's performance issues. And here's something that really hit home for me as a mother—your mitochondrial DNA comes entirely from your mother and passes directly to your children. We're literally creating a health legacy that extends beyond ourselves.

The game-changer? You can apply the same strategic thinking you use to scale your business to optimize your cellular health. Instead of accepting that afternoon energy crash as inevitable, you can actually reset your mitochondria in 28 days using simple daily habits. We're talking about improvements in fat burning, mental clarity, and longevity—not through some complicated biohacking protocol, but through intentional choices around what you eat, how you move, and how you manage stress.

What surprised me most was learning that toxin avoidance and stress management are just as critical as the nutrition and exercise we typically focus on. Your cells are constantly processing not just the food you eat, but the environment you're in and the stress you're carrying. When you optimize all these factors together, you're not just improving your energy—you're literally slowing down the aging process at a cellular level.

This episode will completely change how you think about your daily choices and their impact on your long-term vitality—plus there's a quiz to help you assess your current mitochondrial health.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kristina Kristen

Kristina Kristen is a lifestyle medicine educator, chemistry professor, and author of 10,000 Suns: The 28-Day Mitochondria Reset. She specializes in helping people optimize their cellular health through her NESTS Framework, combining her scientific background with practical wellness strategies.

item.title: When Motherhood Becomes Advocacy: Eden Marie James on Medical Freedom and Change
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest parenting crisis was actually the beginning of your most important leadership calling?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've learned that some of the most powerful leaders aren't born in boardrooms—they're forged in moments when everything they thought they knew gets turned upside down. Eden Marie James's story reminded me why mothers make such formidable advocates: we'll move mountains when our children are at stake.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Personal pain can become your most powerful platform for creating change in the world.
  2. Motherhood often reveals leadership capabilities you never knew you had.
  3. Medical freedom and parental rights require active advocacy from informed mothers.
  4. Grassroots movements led by passionate mothers can drive significant policy change.
  5. Storytelling is one of the most essential tools in advocacy and leadership.
  6. Global issues like child trafficking require local leaders willing to take action.
  7. One woman's courage to speak up can inspire an entire movement of change.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Eden's response wasn't silence — it was action."

The big idea

When Eden Marie James watched her child suffer a vaccine injury, she had a choice: retreat into silence or transform her pain into purpose. She chose the latter, and what happened next is a masterclass in how personal crisis can become your most powerful platform for change.

Eden didn't just become a mother who questioned—she became a mother who acted. She earned her certification as a nutritionist, dove deep into research, and founded a grassroots coalition that's now influencing policy. Her journey proves something I see time and again: motherhood doesn't limit your leadership capacity, it reveals capabilities you never knew existed. The fierce protectiveness we feel for our children becomes the fuel for fighting battles much bigger than ourselves.

What struck me most about Eden's story is how she turned storytelling into activism. She discovered that sharing her truth—even when it was uncomfortable, even when it challenged conventional wisdom—created space for other mothers to find their voices too. One woman's courage to speak up sparked an entire movement of informed parents demanding medical freedom and parental rights.

But Eden's advocacy extends beyond medical freedom. She's tackling issues like child trafficking, proving that when mothers see injustice, we don't stay in our lane—we create new lanes entirely. Her work demonstrates something crucial: global change happens when local leaders decide they're willing to be uncomfortable for the sake of what matters most.

This conversation will challenge you to think differently about advocacy, leadership, and the power of your own story. Whether you're facing a personal crisis or simply feeling called to make a difference, Eden's journey shows that your pain doesn't have to be wasted—it can become the foundation for extraordinary impact. Ready to discover how your own experiences might be preparing you for leadership you never imagined? The full episode breakdown and our exclusive quiz will help you identify your advocacy sweet spot and turn your passion into purposeful action.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Eden Marie James

Eden Marie James is a certified clinical nutritionist, mother, and passionate advocate who founded the MAHA Mom Coalition after her child experienced a vaccine injury. She has spent nearly a decade working globally to combat child trafficking and is a fierce voice for medical freedom and parental rights.

item.title: How a Bipolar Diagnosis Became the Foundation for a Million-Dollar PR Empire
item.content:encoded:
What if your bipolar diagnosis wasn't a career obstacle, but the secret weapon that built your million-dollar empire?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've had countless conversations about turning obstacles into advantages, but Gabrielle Scout Maio's story stopped me in my tracks. She didn't just overcome her bipolar diagnosis—she leveraged it to build emotional intelligence that became the cornerstone of her PR empire. This conversation will change how you see your own challenges.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Mental health challenges can become entrepreneurial superpowers when properly channeled and understood.
  2. Emotional intelligence is more valuable than traditional hustle culture in building sustainable business success.
  3. Being early to innovation is difficult but creates lasting competitive advantages in business.
  4. Motherhood can actually accelerate business performance by forcing efficiency and clarity of priorities.
  5. Authentic leadership that embraces vulnerability creates stronger teams and better business outcomes.
  6. Women leaders naturally expand possibilities for everyone around them through their leadership style.
  7. Your pain has purpose and your story has power when channeled into meaningful work.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Entrepreneurship was her superpower"

The big idea

You know that thing you think disqualifies you from big success? The one you try to hide in networking conversations or downplay in job interviews? Gabrielle Scout Maio is here to flip that script entirely. When she received her bipolar diagnosis, she could have seen it as a limitation. Instead, she recognized it as her entrepreneurial superpower—and built a million-dollar PR empire because of it, not despite it.

Here's what Gabrielle figured out that most of us miss: emotional intelligence beats hustle culture every single time. While everyone else was grinding themselves into the ground, she was developing the kind of deep emotional awareness that comes from navigating mental health challenges. That awareness became her competitive edge. She could read rooms, understand client needs, and build relationships in ways that left her competition scrambling to catch up. Your sensitivity isn't your weakness—it's your business intelligence system.

But this isn't just about reframing challenges. Gabrielle's story reveals something profound about timing and innovation. She was early to understanding that authentic, vulnerable leadership wasn't just nice-to-have—it was business-critical. While others were still playing the old-school power game, she was building teams and client relationships based on genuine connection. Being early to innovation is uncomfortable, but it creates the kind of lasting competitive advantages that can't be copied.

And here's the part that will resonate with so many of you: motherhood didn't slow Gabrielle down—it accelerated her business performance. When you're forced to be efficient, when every minute counts, when you have to get crystal clear on your priorities, magic happens. You stop saying yes to everything and start saying hell yes to the right things. Your capacity doesn't shrink; it gets laser-focused.

The truth Gabrielle lives and breathes is that your pain has purpose and your story has power when you channel it into meaningful work. She's proof that women leaders don't just succeed—we expand possibilities for everyone around us through the way we lead. Ready to discover how your biggest challenge might be your greatest asset? This full conversation will show you exactly how to make that shift, plus there's a quiz that'll help you identify your own hidden entrepreneurial superpowers.

Gabrielle Scout Maio is the founder and CEO of Scouts Agency and author of The Emotional Entrepreneur. item.title: How One Mom With Brain Injury Fought CA Laws Denying 50K Kids School Access
item.content:encoded:
What happens when a mom who can't read aloud discovers 50,000 kids are being denied education?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to share Amy Bohn's story with you because it completely reframes what's possible when we're fighting for something bigger than ourselves. Her journey from struggling with her own brain injury to building a massive advocacy movement proves that our personal battles often prepare us for the fights that matter most.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Recovery from personal challenges can fuel your capacity to fight for others who need a voice.
  2. Organized parent advocacy can successfully defeat restrictive legislation even in hostile political environments.
  3. Understanding the systematic nature of legislative strategies helps you recognize patterns before they impact your family.
  4. Building community around shared values creates exponential impact beyond what any individual can achieve alone.
  5. Speaking up for vulnerable populations often requires stepping outside your comfort zone and into unfamiliar territory.
  6. Legislative changes in one state often serve as testing grounds for nationwide implementation.
  7. Effective advocacy requires both emotional passion and strategic understanding of political processes.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"What started as one exhausted mom showing up at the Capitol became a movement."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you discover something so wrong, so unfair, that you can't just look away? That's exactly what happened to Amy Bohn when she learned that California was systematically denying school access to 50,000 children. Here's the thing that gets me — Amy was dealing with her own brain injury at the time, struggling to read aloud, yet she channeled that challenge into fuel for fighting an entire system.

What Amy built wasn't just impressive, it was strategic. She didn't just get angry and post on social media (though anger has its place). She organized 30,000 parents into a movement that actually defeated restrictive legislation. Think about that for a second — while dealing with her own recovery, she figured out how to navigate political processes, build community around shared values, and create exponential impact that no individual could achieve alone.

This conversation will shift how you think about advocacy because Amy breaks down the systematic nature of how legislation moves from state to state. Once you understand these patterns, you'll start recognizing them everywhere — and that awareness becomes your superpower for protecting what matters to your family before it's too late. She proves that effective advocacy isn't just about emotional passion (though you need that too), it's about understanding how the game is actually played.

What struck me most about Amy's approach is how she stepped completely outside her comfort zone and into unfamiliar territory because vulnerable kids needed someone to speak up. Her story demonstrates that recovery from personal challenges doesn't weaken us — it actually expands our capacity to fight for others who need a voice. If you've ever wondered whether one person can really challenge an entire system, Amy's journey will show you exactly how it's done and inspire you to discover what impact you're capable of creating.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Amy Bohn

Amy Bohn is the President and Co-Founder of PERK (Protection for the Educational Rights of Kids), one of California's most prominent advocates for parental rights and medical freedom. After recovering from a severe brain injury in 2018, she built a grassroots movement of over 30,000 members that has successfully challenged restrictive legislation affecting children's access to education.

item.title: Dr. Holly Wood: Why Pleasure Is Self-Care for Moms, Not Selfishness
item.content:encoded:
What if prioritizing your pleasure wasn't selfish, but actually the most responsible thing you could do as a mother?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've had countless conversations with brilliant women who've somehow convinced themselves that their desires don't matter anymore once they become mothers. When I sat down with Dr. Holly Wood, I knew we were about to blow that myth wide open—and honestly, this conversation left me feeling more validated in my own journey than I expected.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Pleasure is not selfish; it's a vital form of self-care that women deserve to prioritize in their lives.
  2. Motherhood naturally changes your relationship with your body and desire, and accepting this evolution is part of the journey.
  3. Open communication with your partner about your needs and desires is essential for maintaining intimacy through life's seasons.
  4. Understanding that women's desire works differently than men's can help normalize your experience and reduce shame.
  5. Creating safe spaces for pleasure in your relationship requires intentional effort from both partners.
  6. Dismantling shame around sexuality is an ongoing process that benefits your overall well-being.
  7. You have the right to pursue the sex and pleasure you desire, regardless of your life stage or circumstances.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Pleasure isn't selfish. It's self-care."

The big idea

Here's something I wish someone had told me years ago: your pleasure doesn't disappear when you become a mother—it just gets buried under a mountain of everyone else's needs. Dr. Holly Wood, a sexologist who knows exactly what she's talking about, joined me to explore why reclaiming your sexuality isn't just okay, it's essential for your well-being as a woman and a leader.

We talked about how motherhood inevitably changes your relationship with your body and desire, and why that's completely normal. Your body has literally created life—of course things feel different. But different doesn't mean broken, and it certainly doesn't mean you should just accept a life without pleasure. Dr. Wood explained how women's desire works on a completely different timeline than men's, and understanding this can free you from so much unnecessary shame and frustration.

The conversation got real about communication with your partner. You can't expect them to read your mind about what you need, especially when your needs are evolving. Creating those safe spaces for intimacy requires intentional effort from both of you, and it starts with you being willing to advocate for what you actually want. This isn't about being demanding—it's about being honest.

What struck me most was Dr. Wood's point about dismantling shame around sexuality being an ongoing process. We've been conditioned to believe that good mothers sacrifice everything, including their own pleasure. But when you prioritize your well-being—including your sexual well-being—you show up as a more fulfilled, present version of yourself for everyone in your life. You have every right to pursue the intimacy and pleasure you desire, regardless of how many kids you have or what season of life you're in. This episode will challenge everything you think you know about pleasure, responsibility, and what it means to take care of yourself as a woman who leads both at home and in the world.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Holly Wood

Dr. Holly Wood is a sexologist, sex therapist, and women's sexual wellness advocate dedicated to helping women reclaim their sexuality and dismantle shame. She specializes in helping women prioritize pleasure as part of a healthy, fulfilling life.

item.title: From Burnout to Breakthrough: Chef Lindsay Smith's Journey of Resilience
item.content:encoded:
What if running the same business for 19 years isn't settling—it's actually the ultimate masterclass in evolution?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about what real resilience looks like in business, especially after my conversation with Chef Lindsay Smith. Her story completely shifted how I think about longevity, reinvention, and what it means to truly grow alongside your business rather than despite it.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your business should evolve as you evolve—pivot when life demands it rather than forcing yourself to fit an outdated model.
  2. Relationships are the foundation of long-term business success, often more valuable than formal education or credentials.
  3. Learning to delegate and shift from operator to owner creates space for both growth and personal freedom.
  4. Building authentic community in the digital age requires intentional in-real-life connections and vulnerability.
  5. Major life transitions like divorce can become catalysts for business reinvention and personal empowerment.
  6. Creativity often emerges from necessity and constraint, leading to breakthrough innovations like signature menu items.
  7. Middle age and perimenopause can bring unexpected clarity, confidence, and permission to choose differently.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"I realized at my 40th birthday that I couldn't breathe—and I had to make a change"

The big idea

Lindsay Smith didn't just run a restaurant for 19 years—she evolved it, reinvented it, and ultimately used it as the foundation for something completely new. What struck me most about our conversation wasn't just her resilience through divorce, motherhood, and countless industry challenges, but how she refused to let her business become a prison of her own making.

Here's what Lindsay taught me: your business should bend with you, not break you. When life demanded she pivot—whether through personal upheaval or industry shifts—she didn't abandon everything she'd built. Instead, she asked herself a crucial question: how can this business serve the woman I'm becoming, not just the woman I was when I started? That mindset shift is everything. It's the difference between feeling trapped by your success and using it as a launching pad for what's next.

The relationship piece of Lindsay's story hit me hard too. She built her entire business on genuine connections—with suppliers, customers, her team. No fancy degree required, no perfect business plan. Just real relationships and the willingness to show up authentically, even when (especially when) things got messy. Her approach to delegation wasn't about losing control; it was about creating space for the business to grow beyond her individual capacity.

What really gets me is how Lindsay embraced the power of constraint to fuel creativity. Some of her most successful innovations came not from abundance, but from having to figure it out with what she had. And when perimenopause hit? Instead of seeing it as something to endure, she recognized it as a season of unprecedented clarity and permission to choose differently. The full conversation reveals exactly how she navigated each transition and includes a practical assessment to help you identify where your own business might be ready for evolution.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Lindsay Smith

Lindsay Smith is the owner and executive chef of Nirvana by Chef Lindsay, a beloved farm-to-table restaurant in Laguna Beach that she's operated for 19 years. She started her culinary journey at 15 and became a restaurant owner by 26, building not just a business but a community through her monthly ladies' dinner series and commitment to authentic connection.

item.title: Why Money is Freedom: Essential Wealth Strategies Every Woman Needs to Know
item.content:encoded:
Why do 80% of women end up poor in retirement while their male counterparts don't?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about the conversations we're not having about money—the real ones that could change everything. When Melissa Northway started sharing the wealth-building strategies typically reserved for billionaires, I knew this was the conversation every woman in our community needed to hear.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Money equals freedom—the freedom to live life without financial worry and make choices based on values, not limitations.
  2. Women need to take control of their financial planning instead of leaving it to their partners, as statistics show elderly women are disproportionately poor.
  3. Your greatest asset is yourself—protect your earning potential with proper insurance and living benefit riders that provide access to death benefits while alive.
  4. Cash value life insurance functions as a Tier 1 asset with a zero floor, meaning you can't lose money while building tax-advantaged wealth.
  5. Asset swap strategies used by billionaires like Zuckerberg can be adapted for everyday women to optimize tax efficiency.
  6. Developing AI skills can increase income by 10-25%, making it crucial for women to embrace technology for financial advancement.
  7. Approaching money with a 'beginner's mind' and being coachable allows you to learn strategies that can transform your financial future.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Money is freedom—freedom to live the life you want without financial worry"

The big idea

You know that feeling when someone pulls back the curtain on something you always suspected but could never quite name? That's what happened when financial strategist Melissa Northway laid out the stark reality: women are disproportionately ending up poor in retirement, and it's not because we're bad with money. It's because we've been playing by rules that were never designed for us to win.

Here's what Melissa made crystal clear—money isn't about greed or materialism. It's about freedom. The freedom to make choices based on your values instead of your bank account balance. The freedom to leave a bad situation, support causes you believe in, or take that sabbatical without panic. But here's the kicker: while we're busy managing everyone else's needs, we're often leaving our financial futures in someone else's hands. And the statistics are terrifying.

What I loved about this conversation is that Melissa didn't just diagnose the problem—she handed us the playbook. She walked through strategies that billionaires like Zuckerberg use, but adapted them for real women with real lives. We're talking about cash value life insurance as a Tier 1 asset (with a zero floor, meaning you literally cannot lose money), asset swap strategies that optimize your taxes, and why your greatest asset isn't your house or your 401k—it's you. That's why protecting your earning potential isn't just smart, it's non-negotiable.

And can we talk about the AI piece? Melissa shared research showing that developing AI skills can increase your income by 10-25%. While everyone's debating whether technology will replace us, the women who embrace it are already getting paid more. The future belongs to those who show up with what she calls a 'beginner's mind'—ready to learn, ready to adapt, ready to claim their financial power.

This isn't just another money conversation—it's a roadmap to the kind of freedom that changes everything about how you show up in the world, and I can't wait for you to dive into the full episode and discover which wealth-building strategy fits your life right now.

item.title: Why Toxic Resilience Is Keeping You Stuck (And What to Do Instead)
item.content:encoded:
What if the resilience everyone's been praising you for is actually the thing keeping you stuck?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to pause the recording three times during this conversation with Dr. Katie Pritchett because she was calling out patterns I didn't even realize I was living. We've been so conditioned to wear our burnout like a designer handbag that we've forgotten there's actually a different way to lead.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Toxic resilience means wearing burnout as a badge of honor instead of recognizing it as a signal for change.
  2. Regeneration is more powerful than resilience because it allows things to break so you can build something better.
  3. Leadership is neuroscience—you need to understand how your people are wired to lead them effectively.
  4. Women must be involved in creating AI technology because only we can automate the invisible labor we see and do.
  5. Your childhood survival strategies may be running your adult leadership in ways that no longer serve you.
  6. Breaking generational cycles means choosing to inherit healing rather than pain for the next generation.
  7. Real transformation happens through experiential learning, not just consuming more information.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"I don't want him to inherit my pain—I want him to inherit my healing"

The big idea

You know that moment when someone points out something so obvious you can't believe you missed it? That's exactly what happened when Dr. Katie Pritchett explained toxic resilience to me. We've been taught that pushing through, grinding harder, and bouncing back from everything makes us strong leaders. But what if that's actually keeping us trapped in cycles that don't serve us anymore?

Here's the thing about toxic resilience—it's when we wear our burnout as a badge of honor instead of recognizing it as our body's way of saying something needs to change. Dr. Pritchett introduced me to a concept that completely shifted how I think about leadership: regeneration over resilience. Instead of just bouncing back to the same broken system, regeneration allows things to break so you can build something better. It's not about being tougher; it's about being smarter.

We dove deep into how leadership is actually neuroscience—you can't effectively lead people without understanding how they're wired. And here's something that hit me hard: those childhood survival strategies that got you through tough times might be running your adult leadership in ways that no longer serve you. The hypervigilance that kept you safe as a kid could be the same thing making you micromanage your team today.

One of the most powerful parts of our conversation was about breaking generational cycles. Dr. Pritchett reminded me that we have a choice: we can inherit healing instead of pain for the next generation. This isn't just about our children—it's about the women we lead, the teams we build, and the culture we create in our organizations.

But here's what really got me fired up: we talked about how women must be involved in creating AI technology because only we can see and automate the invisible labor that's been on our shoulders for generations. The future of work depends on our perspective, but only if we stop glorifying our burnout long enough to step into that conversation. If you're ready to stop wearing exhaustion as a status symbol and start choosing regeneration over resilience, you need to read the full conversation and take our Toxic Resilience Assessment.

Dr. Katie Pritchett is the founder of KPI Coaching, a leadership development and organizational consulting firm.
