item.title: The Money Story That's Costing Women Millions (And How to Rewrite It)
item.content:encoded:
Your seven-year-old self is controlling your investment decisions—and it's costing you millions.

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think my resistance to certain financial decisions was just being practical. Then Stephanie Guttman helped me trace it back to watching my parents argue about money when I was five. The invisible scripts we inherit about money aren't just limiting our bank accounts—they're shaping entire industries.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your money story is formed by age 7 through interactions with parents, peers, and trusted adults, creating invisible scripts that drive your financial behavior.
  2. The four core money stories are avoidance, money worship, status, and vigilance—with vigilance being the only one correlated with positive financial outcomes.
  3. The investing gap costs women far more long-term than the wage gap because compound interest doubles your money roughly every 10 years.
  4. ETFs allow you to invest in hundreds of stocks with a single purchase, making investing accessible without needing to beat the market.
  5. Teaching children about investing before age 16 triples their likelihood of becoming confident investors as adults.
  6. Women receive only 2-3% of all available capital, making it crucial for women to start investing in women-led businesses and startups.
  7. Financial agency begins with teaching children to make real money decisions, not just theoretical lessons about saving.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The investing gap is costing women far more than the wage gap, and the only way to close it is to stop waiting and start investing."

The big idea

Here's something that will blow your mind: your relationship with money was essentially locked in before you lost your first tooth. Financial expert Stephanie Guttman walked me through how those early interactions—watching your parents stress about bills, hearing 'money doesn't grow on trees,' seeing who had power in financial decisions—created invisible scripts that are running your financial life today. And for most of us, those scripts aren't serving us.

Stephanie breaks down the four core money stories most people operate from: avoidance (money is the root of all evil), money worship (money will solve everything), status (money equals worth), and vigilance (careful, strategic money management). Plot twist? Vigilance is the only one correlated with actual financial success. But here's what really got my attention: the investing gap is costing women far more long-term than the wage gap. While we're fighting for equal pay—and we should be—compound interest is quietly doubling money roughly every ten years for those who are investing. The women who aren't? They're falling further behind exponentially.

The conversation took a turn when we talked about how accessible investing actually is now. ETFs let you buy into hundreds of stocks with a single purchase—you don't need to beat the market or become a financial genius. You just need to start. And if you're a mother, listen up: teaching your kids about investing before age 16 triples their likelihood of becoming confident investors as adults. We're not talking about theoretical piggy bank lessons here. Stephanie advocates for giving children real money decisions to make, real consequences to experience.

But perhaps the most powerful part of our conversation was about women funding women. Right now, women receive only 2-3% of all available capital. That's not just unfair—it's economically stupid. Women-led businesses consistently show strong returns, yet we're systematically underfunded. The solution? Women need to start investing in women-led businesses and startups. We can't wait for the old boys' club to see our value. We need to create our own capital networks. Ready to identify which money story has been running your life and learn how to rewrite it into something that actually builds wealth?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Stephanie Guttman

Stephanie Guttman is the founder of Huntress Wealth, a financial wellness and planning firm dedicated to helping women reclaim confidence with their finances. She hosts the Talk Rich to Me podcast and authors The Arrow, a weekly newsletter helping women align their money with their values.

item.title: 3 Steps to Transform Your Family's Health Without Overwhelm (From a Mom Expert)
item.content:encoded:
What if your 3 AM wake-ups aren't about your bladder, but your liver screaming for help?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been managing teams for decades, but when Julie E. Health broke down how our bodies send us data just like our business dashboards do, everything clicked. She showed me how to read my family's health signals like the CEO I am—and honestly, it's changed how I approach wellness in our household of eight.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your phone is destroying your biofield, but a simple Q-disc sticker can turn it into a healing tool.
  2. The 5-hour head-on-pillow rule rejuvenates glands that regulate all your hormones and can transform perimenopausal symptoms.
  3. Kids with bruises on shins and clumsiness often have weak kidneys affecting their equilibrium.
  4. Waking at specific times reveals which detox organs need support - you're not just waking to pee.
  5. C-section scars can cause back, neck, and shoulder pain years later by disrupting energy meridians.
  6. When your biofield is intact, your body reveals true root causes instead of giving false symptoms.
  7. Fevers in children often indicate toxins in the intestines, and clearing the gut can break the fever quickly.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"When the biofield is intact, healing is limitless."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you finally crack a code that's been frustrating you for months? That's exactly what happened when I sat down with Julie E. Health to talk about managing family wellness without the overwhelm that usually comes with it. Julie shared her three-step framework that treats your body like the intelligent system it is—and trust me, this isn't your typical 'eat more vegetables' advice.

Here's what blew my mind: Julie explained how your wake-up times are actually data points telling you exactly which organs need support. Waking between 1-3 AM? Your liver is working overtime. Up at 5 AM consistently? Your large intestine is flagging for attention. It's like having a built-in health dashboard, but most of us have never learned to read it. She also revealed how something as simple as a Q-disc sticker on your phone can transform it from an energy disruptor into a healing tool—because let's be real, none of us are giving up our devices.

The conversation got really practical when Julie talked about protecting your biofield (think of it as your body's energy management system) and supporting your detox organs. She shared why that 5-hour head-on-pillow rule isn't just about sleep, but about giving your hormone-regulating glands the restoration time they desperately need. For those of us navigating perimenopause while running companies and families, this was pure gold.

What I loved most was how Julie connected the dots between things we often dismiss—like kids being clumsy or having bruised shins (hello, kidney support needed), or how C-section scars can cause shoulder pain years later by disrupting energy meridians. When your body's energy system is intact, Julie explained, you stop getting false symptoms and start seeing the real root causes. Ready to decode what your family's bodies are actually telling you and take the wellness quiz that'll show you exactly where to start?

item.title: Why 6,988 Pages of the Tax Code Are Written to Help You Pay Less
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that 6,988 pages of the tax code exist specifically to help you pay less?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think taxes were just something I had to endure as a business owner — until I met Barbara Schreihans. She completely flipped my understanding of how the tax system actually works, and honestly, I wish I'd had this conversation years ago when I was leaving money on the table every single year.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Only 12 pages of the 7,000-page tax code tell you what to pay — the other 6,988 pages show you how to pay less.
  2. S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited than sole proprietorships and can save significant self-employment taxes.
  3. Switching to an S-Corp can save you over $6,000 annually even on just $40,000 of income.
  4. Women have higher investment returns than men but invest less money overall.
  5. Your accountant should explain how to take deductions legally, not just say no to everything.
  6. Strategic December car purchases can result in 100% tax write-offs for business vehicles.
  7. Regular money conversations with your spouse prevent financial conflicts and build wealth together.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The tax code is a roadmap for entrepreneurs, not a punishment"

The big idea

Here's what blew my mind: out of the 7,000-page tax code, only 12 pages tell you what you owe. The remaining 6,988 pages? They're literally a roadmap showing you how to pay less. Yet most of us entrepreneurs are walking around overpaying because we think taxes are just this inevitable burden we have to bear.

Barbara breaks down why your business structure matters more than you think. If you're still operating as a sole proprietorship, you're 35 times more likely to get audited than if you switch to an S-Corp. Even more compelling? That switch can save you over $6,000 annually on just $40,000 of income. We're talking real money here, not theoretical savings that sound good on paper but don't actually move the needle.

What really frustrated me during our conversation was realizing how many accountants are doing their clients a disservice by defaulting to 'no' on deductions instead of explaining how to take them legally. Your accountant should be your strategic partner in wealth building, not someone who just files your paperwork and calls it a day. Barbara shares specific strategies like timing major purchases in December for maximum write-offs and how to structure your business expenses to work in your favor.

We also dove into something that hits close to home for many of us: the investment gap. Women consistently outperform men in investment returns, yet we invest less money overall. It's not about our ability — it's about our approach and often our confidence in these financial decisions. Barbara and I talked through how regular money conversations with your spouse can transform not just your relationship but your entire wealth-building strategy.

This conversation is packed with actionable strategies that could literally save you thousands this year, plus there's a quiz at the end that'll help you figure out exactly which business structure makes the most sense for where you are right now.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Barbara Schreihans

Barbara Schreihans is the Founder and CEO of Your Tax Coach, creator of the Write Off Your Life™ Course, and a nationally recognized tax strategist who helps entrepreneurs save tens of millions in taxes while growing their profits.

item.title: How Love and Oneness Unlock Million-Dollar Business Success with Dave Lundgren
item.content:encoded:
What if quantum physics just proved that love isn't soft leadership—it's the hardest business strategy you'll ever master?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've built companies worth hundreds of millions, but this conversation with Dave Lundgren shifted something fundamental in how I think about success. When someone who's helped build billion-dollar empires alongside Tony Robbins tells you that love and oneness are universal laws—not just feel-good concepts—you listen.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Love and Oneness are universal laws backed by both quantum physics and spirituality that drive true business success.
  2. Top entrepreneurs tap into feminine energy for visionary breakthroughs while using masculine energy for execution and structure.
  3. Women need approximately 20% masculine energy to effectively scale their businesses and lead with authority.
  4. Authenticity is your most powerful marketing tool because it allows you to genuinely connect with your ideal audience.
  5. Real estate investing offers four returns on every dollar invested, making it superior to traditional stock market investing.
  6. AI should be used as a tool to free you to be more human, not replace human consciousness and connection.
  7. The measure of mastery in leadership is how quickly you can recover from setbacks and challenges.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The false sense of separation is the root cause of most human suffering in business and life."

The big idea

You've been told that business is about hustle, competition, and playing hardball. But what if everything you've learned about scaling to seven and eight figures is missing the most powerful piece? Dave Lundgren didn't just theorize about love and oneness—he used these principles to build billion-dollar companies, and the science backs him up completely.

Here's what's fascinating: quantum physics confirms that everything is interconnected, which means when you operate from genuine love and connection, you're literally working with universal laws instead of against them. But this isn't about going soft—it's about understanding that you need both feminine visionary energy AND masculine execution energy to scale. Dave breaks down why women specifically need about 20% masculine energy to lead with the authority your business demands, while staying rooted in the intuitive, connective strengths that make you unstoppable.

The conversation gets even more practical when we dive into why authenticity isn't just a buzzword—it's your most powerful marketing tool because it creates genuine magnetic connection with your ideal clients. Dave also shares why real estate beats stock market investing hands down, offering four different returns on every dollar you invest, and how to use AI as a tool to become more human, not less connected.

But perhaps the most powerful insight? True leadership mastery isn't about never failing—it's about how quickly you can recover and pivot when challenges hit. Dave reveals exactly how the most successful entrepreneurs use both love and strategic thinking to bounce back faster and stronger than their competition. Ready to discover how these universal laws can transform your business and take our quiz to see which energy you're leading with?

item.title: Why Writing Your Book Alone Is Holding You Back: The Power of Book Midwives
item.description: Episode 47 | with Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski
item.content:encoded:
What if the secret to finally writing your book isn't more discipline, but finding your book midwives?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been sitting on a book idea for years, telling myself I needed better time management or more willpower. Then I met Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski—my book midwives—and everything changed. This conversation will shift how you think about bringing your story into the world.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Writing is an embodied act that requires community, nourishment, and permission to be messy.
  2. The defining question for any manuscript is: 'What absolutely has to be in this book?'
  3. Women write differently than men and need to honor their unique creative processes rather than forcing traditional methods.
  4. Your body knows your story before your mind does, so listen to what wants to emerge.
  5. There's never a perfect time to write a book, just like having a baby or getting a puppy.
  6. Legacy authors don't just write one book—they step into writing as part of their identity.
  7. The people you least expect will be most transformed by your words, so don't hold back your story.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"What absolutely has to be in this book? That question changed everything for my writing process."

The big idea

You know that book that's been living inside you? The one you keep promising yourself you'll write when you have more time, more clarity, more confidence? Here's what I learned that will change everything: writing a book isn't about forcing yourself into someone else's process. It's about honoring how you, as a woman, naturally create.

Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski opened my eyes to something revolutionary—writing is an embodied act. Your body knows your story before your mind does. Instead of sitting at your computer trying to force words onto a page, what if you trusted what wants to emerge? What if you stopped apologizing for needing community, nourishment, and yes, permission to be messy in your creative process? The traditional 'just sit down and write' advice wasn't designed for how women create, and it's time we stopped pretending it works for us.

The question that changed everything for me was this: 'What absolutely has to be in this book?' Not what you think people want to hear, not what sounds impressive, but what absolutely must be shared. When you start there, everything shifts. You stop overthinking and start trusting. You realize there's never a perfect time to write a book—just like there's never a perfect time to have a baby or get a puppy—but the world needs what you have to say.

Here's what hit me hardest: the people you least expect will be most transformed by your words. That story you're holding back because you think it's too personal, too messy, too ordinary? That's exactly the story someone needs to hear. Legacy authors don't just write one book—they step into writing as part of their identity. They find their book midwives, the people who believe in their message and help bring it to life with the care and attention it deserves. Ready to discover your unique creative process and finally birth that book that's been waiting inside you?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski

Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski are the co-founders of Synergy Publishing Group and creators of the Rebel Writers program. These 'book midwives' combine decades of writing expertise with a revolutionary approach to helping mission-driven women birth their books through community, embodiment, and retreat experiences.

item.title: 50 Years Behind the Camera: What Media Insiders Don't Want You to Know
item.content:encoded:
What if fifty years behind the camera taught one broadcaster the secret that could transform how you lead today?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I sat down with legendary broadcaster Steve Edwards, and honestly, I wasn't prepared for how much his five decades in media would teach me about authentic leadership. His insights on staying true to yourself while constantly evolving hit me right where I needed to hear it.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Gratitude is a daily superpower that can combat negative mental spirals and transform your perspective on challenges.
  2. Career longevity requires constant reinvention while staying true to your authentic self rather than performing a character.
  3. Social media is having unprecedented negative impacts on young people, particularly girls, that we haven't fully grasped yet.
  4. The internet and cable news have accelerated tribalism in ways our society hasn't caught up to, fragmenting our shared reality.
  5. Authenticity beats performance every single time, whether you're on camera or in any leadership role.
  6. Staying relevant in any career means never stopping your curiosity and willingness to learn and adapt.
  7. Breaking news coverage requires balancing truth-telling with responsibility for the audience's emotional wellbeing.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Gratitude will get you through anything - it's a daily practice that can change your entire perspective"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're trying to be the 'right' kind of leader — polished, perfect, always having the answers? Steve Edwards just blew that myth to pieces. After fifty years in the media spotlight, from local news to national television, he's learned something most of us are still figuring out: authenticity isn't just nice to have, it's your competitive advantage.

Here's what struck me most about our conversation — Steve talked about gratitude as a daily superpower, not some fluffy self-help concept. When you're leading through chaos (and let's be honest, when aren't we?), that practice of deliberately shifting your perspective becomes the difference between spiraling and thriving. He's seen it all behind the camera, from breaking news that shakes communities to the pressure of staying relevant in an industry that changes overnight. Yet he's managed to reinvent himself repeatedly without losing who he is at his core.

The conversation took a sobering turn when we discussed what social media is doing to our daughters. As someone raising six kids in this digital world, Steve's observations about the unprecedented impact on young girls hit close to home. But here's what I appreciated — he didn't just point out the problem, he showed how authentic leadership can be part of the solution. When you lead with genuine curiosity instead of performing a character, you create space for real connection in a world that's increasingly fragmented.

What really got me thinking was Steve's insight about career longevity. You might think someone with his track record would have it all figured out, but he emphasized that staying relevant means never stopping your willingness to learn and adapt. It's not about chasing every trend — it's about maintaining that curiosity that keeps you growing while staying anchored in your authentic self. Whether you're on camera, in the boardroom, or managing your team, that balance between evolution and authenticity is what separates leaders who last from those who burn out.

This episode challenged me to think differently about how we show up as leaders in an age of information overload and tribal thinking, and I have a feeling it's going to do the same for you.

About Steve Edwards

Steve Edwards is a legendary Los Angeles broadcaster with over 50 years in television, including hosting Good Day LA for 17 years. He has received the Governor's Award from the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences and has a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

item.title: How One Mom's 'Silly' Trivia Business Became a Community Empire
item.content:encoded:
What if your 'silly' business idea is actually the smartest move you could make right now?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to smile when Christina Wooldridge described her trivia business as 'silly' — because aren't we all guilty of diminishing our own brilliant ideas? This conversation reminded me why the businesses we almost talk ourselves out of are often the ones the world needs most.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Hospitality isn't a soft skill—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation.
  2. Your business idea doesn't need to be prestigious to be valuable and profitable.
  3. Human connection, not just products or services, is what keeps customers coming back long-term.
  4. Scaling doesn't always mean doing more—sometimes it means packaging your expertise differently.
  5. Motherhood sharpens leadership skills through forced intentionality and laser focus.
  6. The real value in business comes from creating belonging and community, not just transactions.
  7. Unconventional ideas often succeed because they solve real human needs that others overlook.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Hospitality is not a soft skill — it's a leadership superpower"

The big idea

You know that business idea rattling around in your head? The one you keep dismissing because it doesn't sound 'serious' enough? Christina Wooldridge's story might just change how you think about it. She turned what she called a 'silly' trivia concept into a thriving community empire — and her journey reveals something powerful about what really drives business success.

Here's what struck me most about Christina's approach: she discovered that hospitality isn't some soft, fluffy skill — it's actually a leadership superpower. When you can make people feel genuinely seen and valued in any conversation, you create real power. Not the kind that comes from titles or credentials, but the kind that comes from human connection. Christina built her entire business model around this principle, and it's why her customers don't just buy from her — they belong with her.

The brilliance in Christina's story isn't just about trivia nights. It's about recognizing that your business idea doesn't need a prestigious pedigree to be valuable and profitable. Sometimes the most unconventional ideas succeed precisely because they solve real human needs that everyone else is overlooking. While others chase complex solutions, Christina focused on something beautifully simple: creating spaces where people feel like they belong.

As a mother of six, I was fascinated by Christina's insights on how motherhood actually sharpened her leadership skills. The forced intentionality, the laser focus, the ability to make decisions quickly — these aren't obstacles to overcome in business, they're advantages to leverage. She also redefined what scaling means, showing that sometimes growth isn't about doing more, but about packaging your expertise differently.

What Christina really built wasn't a trivia business — it was a community empire based on the understanding that people don't just want transactions, they want transformation through connection. Her success proves that the real value in business often comes from creating belonging, not just delivering products or services. Ready to discover how to turn your own 'silly' idea into something serious? Dive into the full conversation and take our quiz to uncover your own community-building superpower.

item.title: Why Intentional Relationships Are the Ultimate Business Strategy
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you the one business skill AI can never replace is sitting in your phone's contact list right now?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've built a nine-figure company, but honestly? The most valuable asset isn't our tech or our team structure—it's the intentional relationships I've cultivated over decades. When Scott MacGregor shared his 'extra inch' philosophy, something clicked about why some leaders thrive while others plateau.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Stop trying to figure out the end result and just pour into people with genuine intention.
  2. Going the 'extra inch' with 5 seconds of additional effort creates 100x the impact in relationships.
  3. Use your phone's contact notes as a simple but powerful CRM to track meaningful details about people.
  4. Shift from networking to friendship building by genuinely caring about people as humans first.
  5. Relationships follow unpredictable paths, so trust the process rather than forcing outcomes.
  6. Choose impact over income because people remember how you made them feel, not your revenue.
  7. Daily relationship building, like athletic training, strengthens your connection muscles over time.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Scale can steal the soul"

The big idea

You know that moment when someone remembers your kid's name or follows up on something you mentioned months ago? That's not luck—that's intentional relationship building, and it's the secret weapon Scott MacGregor uses to create business magic that no algorithm can replicate.

Here's what hit me hardest in our conversation: we're so obsessed with optimizing our sales funnels and growth strategies that we're missing the most powerful business tool we have—genuine human connection. Scott's 'extra inch' concept is brilliantly simple: those five extra seconds you spend adding a personal note, remembering a detail, or checking in without an agenda create 100 times the impact of any marketing campaign you'll ever run.

But here's where most of us go wrong—we're trying to engineer relationships like we engineer products. We want to know the ROI, the timeline, the guaranteed outcome. Scott flips this completely. Stop calculating. Start caring. Use your phone's contact notes like a simple CRM for humanity. When someone mentions their daughter's soccer tournament, note it. When they're stressed about a project, follow up. When they celebrate a win, remember it.

The shift from networking to friendship building changes everything. People don't do business with companies—they do business with people they trust, people who see them as humans first. And trust me, as someone managing six kids and a massive company, I know the power of choosing impact over income. People forget your revenue numbers, but they never forget how you made them feel.

Think of relationship building like training for a marathon—you can't cram authentic connections into a weekend workshop. It's daily practice, consistent investment, and genuine care compounded over time. The leaders who understand this don't just build businesses; they build legacies. Ready to discover exactly how Scott's philosophy can transform your approach to leadership and unlock opportunities you never saw coming?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Scott MacGregor

Scott MacGregor is an entrepreneur, publisher, and 4x author with 20+ years in sales leadership. As founder of The Outlier Project and Editor-in-Chief of Outlier Magazine, he champions authentic relationship building over traditional networking.

item.title: The $10 Trillion Job Every Mother Does for Free (And Why It's Time to Name It)
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that you're already working a job worth $10 trillion—but doing it for free?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to pause the recording three times during this conversation with Dr. Danielle Dowling because what she was saying hit so close to home. As someone running a nine-figure company while raising six kids, I thought I had figured out the balance. Turns out, I was still carrying invisible weight I didn't even know I was holding.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Naming motherhood as work is not a betrayal of love—it's an act of dignity and self-worth.
  2. Women globally perform 12.5 billion hours of unpaid labor daily, valued at over $10 trillion annually.
  3. Self-abandonment disguised as virtue is still self-abandonment and erases women's identities.
  4. Replace asking for 'help' with asking partners to 'own' responsibilities to transfer real accountability.
  5. The progression of self-worth to self-respect to self-care creates sustainable boundaries and wellness.
  6. Good girl conditioning transforms systemic overwhelm into personal shame instead of warranted critique.
  7. Raising independent children requires mothers to stop doing everything for them and teach responsibility.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Naming motherhood as work is not a betrayal of love—it's an act of dignity."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're lying in bed at 11 PM, mentally running through tomorrow's schedule while also remembering that you need to buy new soccer cleats, schedule that dentist appointment, and somehow figure out what's for dinner? That mental load you carry isn't just part of being a good mom—it's unpaid labor worth over $10 trillion globally. And it's time we stopped treating it like it's just what women naturally do.

Dr. Danielle Dowling breaks down why naming motherhood as work isn't about diminishing the love we have for our families—it's about reclaiming our dignity and self-worth. When we perform 12.5 billion hours of unpaid labor daily and then feel guilty for being exhausted, we're not experiencing personal failure. We're experiencing the natural result of carrying an impossible load that society has conditioned us to see as normal.

The shift starts with language. Instead of asking your partner to 'help' with household responsibilities, you ask them to 'own' specific areas. Help implies it's still your job and they're doing you a favor. Ownership transfers actual accountability. This isn't about keeping score—it's about creating sustainable systems that don't require you to abandon yourself in service of everyone else.

What really struck me was Danielle's insight about self-abandonment disguised as virtue. When we pride ourselves on doing everything for everyone, we're not just burning out—we're teaching our children that this is what love looks like. We're modeling that women disappear into their roles instead of thriving within them. The progression she outlines from self-worth to self-respect to self-care isn't selfish—it's essential for raising independent children who understand that everyone in a family has both privileges and responsibilities.

This conversation will challenge everything you've been told about what makes a good mother, and give you permission to value your contribution in a whole new way. Read the full breakdown to discover how to break the cycle of good girl conditioning and take our quiz to identify where you might be abandoning yourself without even realizing it.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Danielle Dowling

Dr. Danielle Dowling, Psy.D. is a Los Angeles-based certified life coach and doctor of psychology with over 14 years and 15,000+ hours of coaching experience. She helps high-achieving women and mothers reconnect with their self-worth and build lives they genuinely feel proud of. She is the author of the upcoming book Good Girl, Bad Mom.

item.title: From Divorce to Six-Figure Success: How One Mom Rewrote Her Financial Story
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest financial mistake isn't spending too much—but making yourself financially dependent on someone else?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I sat down with Tiana Reeves because her story completely reframes what it means to build wealth as a mom. She went from financial dependence through divorce to six-figure success, and the way she thinks about money, purpose, and peace will shift something in you.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Rich isn't about income—it's about joy, peace, and the memories you create with your family.
  2. Teaching children financial literacy early using savings, giving, and spending buckets sets them up for lifelong success.
  3. Imposter syndrome often means you haven't found your true purpose yet, not that you're inadequate.
  4. Marriage should be treated like a corporate merger—make sure your values and goals align before committing.
  5. Financial dependence costs more than money—it costs your peace, autonomy, and sense of self-worth.
  6. Starting with what you enjoy naturally leads to finding your purpose and financial success.
  7. Peace in your household is worth more than any amount of gold or material wealth.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Marriage is a corporate merger — and divorce is a divestiture. Make sure your corporate cultures align before you merge."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about financial independence: it's not really about the money. Tiana Reeves learned this the hard way when her marriage ended and she found herself financially dependent with children to support. But instead of staying stuck in that vulnerable position, she did something extraordinary—she passed her PMP certification in just six weeks and completely rewrote her relationship with money.

The real revelation in our conversation wasn't about her impressive career pivot, though. It was how Tiana redefined what 'rich' actually means. She's teaching her kids—and frankly, teaching all of us—that wealth isn't about your income number. It's about joy, peace, and the memories you create with your family. She uses a simple system of savings, giving, and spending buckets with her children that's so practical you'll want to start it tonight.

But here's where Tiana's story gets really interesting: she discovered that imposter syndrome isn't actually about being inadequate. It's often a signal that you haven't found your true purpose yet. When she started with what she naturally enjoyed, everything else—including the financial success—followed. She's built an entire movement for corporate moms by understanding this principle.

The conversation took a bold turn when Tiana shared her perspective on marriage as a corporate merger. Before you roll your eyes, hear me out—she's talking about making sure your values and goals actually align before you commit. Because financial dependence doesn't just cost you money. It costs your peace, your autonomy, and your sense of self-worth. And as Tiana puts it, peace in your household is worth more than any amount of gold.

This episode will make you rethink everything about how you approach money, purpose, and the kind of security you're really building for yourself and your family. Ready to discover which financial archetype you are and how it's shaping your relationship with money?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Tiana Reeves

Tiana Reeves is a PMP-certified project manager, Six Sigma Black Belt, and creator of the Corporate Mom Movement. She transformed her life from pregnancy loss, divorce, and corporate burnout into becoming a bestselling author and advocate for working mothers' financial independence.

item.title: From Frustrated Mom to Political Candidate: When Parental Rights Meet Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What if your mama bear instincts are actually the exact leadership skills our broken political system desperately needs?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've had countless conversations with brilliant women who feel completely powerless watching what's happening in their kids' schools. But Kristina Irwin did something most of us only dream about—she channeled that frustration into running for state senate. Her story completely shifted how I think about parental advocacy and political leadership.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Motherhood provides a masterclass in leadership skills that translate directly to political and community leadership.
  2. Parents have the right and responsibility to know what their children are being taught in schools.
  3. Write-in campaigns can be surprisingly effective when you have genuine grassroots support and a clear message.
  4. Education reform should focus on teaching practical skills like AI literacy and real-world preparation rather than ideology.
  5. Breaking political supermajorities requires common sense candidates willing to put principles over party politics.
  6. When you feel powerless about your children's future, channel that frustration into actionable change rather than helpless anger.
  7. Parental rights issues cross party lines and represent a fundamental women's issue about protecting our children.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Public education has become indoctrination"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're watching something happen to your children and every fiber of your being wants to fix it, but you feel completely powerless? That's exactly where Kristina Irwin found herself during COVID lockdowns when she discovered what was really being taught in her kids' classrooms. But here's what makes Kristina different from most frustrated parents: instead of just complaining at PTA meetings or venting to other moms, she decided to run for state senate as a write-in candidate.

What struck me most about Kristina's journey is how she recognized that all those skills we use as mothers—managing competing priorities, mediating conflicts, making decisions with incomplete information, rallying people around a common cause—these aren't just parenting skills. They're executive leadership skills that translate directly into political effectiveness. She didn't see her lack of traditional political experience as a weakness; she saw her motherhood as her greatest qualification.

The conversation gets really practical when Kristina breaks down how she built a genuine grassroots campaign from scratch. We're talking about someone who had never run for office before, taking on established political machines with nothing but a clear message about parental rights and common-sense education reform. She shares exactly how she identified her base of support, why write-in campaigns can actually be more powerful than traditional races, and how she navigated the challenge of staying principle-centered in a highly partisan environment.

But this isn't just a story about one woman's political campaign. Kristina's experience reveals something crucial about how we can transform our frustration into meaningful action, whether that's in politics, our communities, or our careers. She talks about why parental rights issues are fundamentally women's issues, how education reform should focus on preparing kids for real-world challenges like AI literacy instead of pushing ideological agendas, and why breaking political supermajorities requires candidates willing to put principles over party politics. If you've ever felt that familiar rage about your children's future and wondered how to channel it into something productive, you'll want to hear exactly how Kristina turned her mama bear energy into a blueprint for systemic change.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kristina Irwin

Kristina Irwin is a mother, first-generation immigrant, and candidate for California State Senate District 24. She earned 6,300 write-in votes in 2022 after being awakened to educational concerns during COVID lockdowns and is now running on the official 2026 ballot on a platform of parental rights, property rights, and public safety.

item.title: Why Modern Women Are 'Out of Balance' and How to Return to Your Feminine Nature
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you've been taught about succeeding as a woman is actually burning you out?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had one of those conversations with Chelsea Ann O'Brien that left me questioning everything I thought I knew about balance. As someone who's built a nine-figure company while raising six kids, I thought I had this figured out. Turns out, there's so much more to unpack about what it really means to thrive as a woman in today's world.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Modern women are 'out of their nature' due to conditioning that pushes them into masculine energy patterns.
  2. The father wound drives who you date, while the mother wound drives how you mother your children.
  3. Motherhood isn't an identity to grasp but a gift that exists beyond the ego.
  4. Healing requires feeling your emotions, not thinking your way through them.
  5. Women don't need to become like men to access their power and succeed.
  6. Returning to feminine nature starts with creating space from societal conditioning.
  7. Redefining success means basing it on your authentic nature rather than external validation.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Women don't need to become like men to be powerful"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're crushing it professionally but something still feels... off? Chelsea Ann O'Brien gets it, and in our conversation, she reveals why so many high-achieving women are secretly struggling. It's not about time management or better systems—it's about something much deeper. We've been conditioned to operate in masculine energy patterns that are fundamentally out of alignment with our nature as women.

Chelsea's journey from extreme wealth to spiritual surrender opened my eyes to how the wounds we carry shape everything from our dating patterns to our parenting style. The father wound influences who we're attracted to and how we show up in relationships, while the mother wound drives our approach to raising our children. But here's what hit me hardest: motherhood isn't an identity to grasp onto—it's a gift that exists beyond our ego's need to control and define everything.

What really shifted my perspective was Chelsea's insight about healing. We can't think our way through emotional healing—we have to actually feel our way through it. As women who've learned to strategize and optimize everything, this feels counterintuitive. But the path back to our authentic power doesn't require us to become more like men or push harder in masculine ways. It requires us to create space from the societal conditioning that told us our natural way of being wasn't enough.

The conversation took an unexpected turn when Chelsea shared her insights about the massive shift she sees coming in 2027 and how it will impact women specifically. She's helping women redefine success based on their authentic nature rather than external validation, and honestly, it's revolutionary. If you've been feeling like something's missing despite all your achievements, you need to hear what Chelsea shared about returning to your feminine nature while still building the life and career you want.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Chelsea Ann O'Brien

Chelsea Ann O'Brien is a board-certified family nurse practitioner and author of five books including The Feminine Effect and Mystical Motherhood. She's a modern healer who studied with renowned midwife Ina May Gaskin and practices at The Morrison Center in NYC, dedicated to helping women remember their true nature.

item.title: Why Parenting Is Leadership (And How to Stop Doing Everything Yourself)
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that managing your household makes you more qualified to lead than most executives?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to pause this conversation with Kristina Campos three times because she kept dropping truth bombs that made me rethink everything about motherhood. Her story from crying on a closet floor to building a parenting empire isn't just inspiring—it's a masterclass in recognizing the leadership skills you already have.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your home is a leadership academy where you practice systems management, delegation, and domestic leadership daily.
  2. Playing the long game means investing in teaching skills now for independence later rather than doing everything yourself.
  3. Fifteen minutes of one-on-one time with each child daily creates transformational connection and communication.
  4. Technology should be navigated and scaffolded rather than eliminated, teaching kids to manage screen time responsibly.
  5. The hardest seasons in life often become incubators for your next chapter and greatest purpose.
  6. Accepting your child's authentic self during identity exploration strengthens your relationship long-term.
  7. Co-parenting after divorce works best when you play to each other's strengths rather than competing.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You may have given a life, but it's not your life to live"

The big idea

Your morning routine alone proves you're already a leader. Think about it: you're managing multiple schedules, delegating tasks, problem-solving in real time, and motivating your team (aka your family) to perform. Yet somehow we've been conditioned to see parenting as separate from leadership, when in reality, your home is the most complex organization you'll ever run.

Kristina Campos learned this the hard way during her own closet floor breakdown, but what she discovered next changed everything. Instead of continuing to do everything herself—the dishes, the laundry, the endless mental load—she started playing the long game. She began investing fifteen minutes of focused one-on-one time with each child daily, not as another task on her list, but as CEO-to-future-CEO mentoring sessions. The result? Kids who felt truly seen and heard, and a mother who finally understood that teaching skills now means freedom later.

This isn't about creating mini-adults or losing the magic of childhood. It's about recognizing that when you scaffold your children's growth—whether it's navigating technology responsibly or accepting their authentic selves during identity exploration—you're practicing advanced leadership skills that most executives never master. You're learning to delegate without micromanaging, to set systems that work without your constant oversight, and to play to each person's strengths rather than forcing everyone into the same mold.

The conversation gets especially real when Kristina shares how her hardest seasons became the incubator for her greatest purpose, and why co-parenting after divorce actually taught her more about true partnership than her marriage ever did. What struck me most was her insight that the very skills we dismiss as 'just parenting' are exactly what the business world desperately needs more of: authentic connection, long-term thinking, and the ability to develop others instead of just getting things done. Ready to see your parenting through a completely different lens and discover just how qualified you already are to lead anything?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kristina Campos

Kristina Campos is the founder of The Impactful Parent with 20+ years of classroom experience from Pre-K through 12th grade. A mother of four and parenting educator, she created a coaching practice, podcast, and free parenting app after rebuilding her life following divorce.

item.title: Why Self-Care Isn't Selfish: Dr. Laura Brayton on Holistic Motherhood
item.content:encoded:
What if taking care of yourself isn't selfish—it's the most important business decision you'll ever make?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think self-care was something I'd get to 'someday' when I had more time. Then I realized that my well-being isn't separate from my success—it's the foundation of everything else. Dr. Laura Brayton completely shifted how I think about motherhood, leadership, and the non-negotiable investment in ourselves.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Self-care is not selfish—it's mandatory for every mother because everyone benefits when mom is well.
  2. Chiropractic care is safe throughout all three trimesters and can significantly improve pregnancy outcomes.
  3. Preconception detox should begin six months before conception for both partners to optimize fertility and baby's health.
  4. Your nervous system must be regulated to lead from intuition rather than fear-based decisions.
  5. Life is written in pencil—you have permission to pivot your career to align with motherhood.
  6. Being fully present for one hour daily gives your children more attention than most kids receive.
  7. Corporate skills translate beautifully into household leadership when you embrace the shift in energy.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Self-care is not selfish—it is mandatory for every mother"

The big idea

Here's what no one tells you about motherhood: the moment you become a mom, everyone expects you to put yourself last. But Dr. Laura Brayton is here to flip that script entirely. In our conversation, she made it crystal clear that self-care isn't a luxury you squeeze in between everything else—it's mandatory infrastructure for the life you're building.

Think about it this way: when you're on an airplane, they tell you to put your own oxygen mask on first. That's not selfishness—that's strategy. Laura breaks down why your nervous system regulation directly impacts your ability to lead from intuition instead of fear, and how that ripple effect touches every decision you make in business and at home. She's not talking about bubble baths and face masks (though those count too). She's talking about the deep work of detoxing your body six months before conception, getting chiropractic care throughout pregnancy, and recognizing that your physical health is your leadership foundation.

What hit me hardest was her permission to pivot. Life is written in pencil, she says, and you get to rewrite your career story to align with motherhood. Those corporate skills you've spent years developing? They don't disappear—they transform into household leadership superpowers. The energy shifts, but the competence remains. Laura also destroys the guilt around quality time with a game-changing insight: being fully present for one focused hour daily gives your children more attention than most kids receive all week.

This isn't about doing more—it's about doing differently. Laura's approach to holistic motherhood recognizes that when mom is well, everyone wins. Your family, your business, your impact—it all starts with you taking care of the person who takes care of everyone else. Ready to discover exactly how to make self-care your non-negotiable foundation and find out which type of leader-mom you are?

item.title: Why Parenting and Leadership Are the Same Skill Set (According to a Psychologist)
item.content:encoded:
What if gentle parenting created exactly the opposite of what we intended for our kids and our teams?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been wrestling with this question for months as I watch both my kids and my employees struggle with boundaries and expectations. When Dr. Andrea Mata told me that parenting and leadership are literally the same skill set, everything clicked into place.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Authoritative parenting combines high expectations with high support, creating the best outcomes for children and employees.
  2. Gentle parenting swung too far from authoritarian into permissiveness, creating a generation struggling with adaptability.
  3. It costs twice an employee's salary to fire and replace them, making coaching the smarter financial investment.
  4. Gen Z employees need clear behavioral expectations and frequent check-ins, not vague annual reviews.
  5. Seventy-one percent of household cognitive labor falls on mothers, creating an unsustainable mental load.
  6. Motherhood is leadership training for every future relationship your child will have.
  7. You don't have to be perfect as a parent or leader—you have to be present and consistent.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Parenting your child IS modeling leadership for every future relationship"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're trying to be the "cool mom" who never says no, and suddenly your house is chaos and your kids are calling the shots? Turns out, the same thing is happening in workplaces across America, and it's creating a generation that can't handle feedback, boundaries, or real-world expectations.

Dr. Andrea Mata, a clinical psychologist who's studied both family dynamics and organizational behavior, dropped some hard truths in our conversation that honestly made me rethink everything. She explained how gentle parenting—which started as a beautiful correction to authoritarian, fear-based parenting—swung so far in the opposite direction that we're now raising kids who crumble at the first sign of disappointment. And here's the kicker: we're managing our teams the exact same way, avoiding difficult conversations and clear expectations because we don't want to be seen as "mean."

The solution isn't going back to old-school authoritarian leadership (thank God), but embracing what psychologists call authoritative parenting and leadership. This means high expectations paired with high support—you set clear standards AND you give people the tools and encouragement to meet them. Whether you're dealing with a teenager who won't do their chores or an employee who's consistently missing deadlines, the approach is identical: clear consequences, consistent follow-through, and genuine investment in their growth.

What really got me was when Andrea shared that 71% of household cognitive labor still falls on mothers—we're already doing the invisible work of managing, organizing, and keeping everyone on track. These aren't just "mom skills"—they're executive functions that directly translate to leadership success. The ability to see around corners, anticipate needs, manage multiple priorities, and develop people's potential? That's exactly what great leaders do.

The most liberating part of our conversation was when Andrea said you don't have to be perfect as a parent or leader—you just have to be present and consistent. Stop trying to be everyone's best friend and start being the leader your kids and your team actually need. Ready to find out which leadership style you're actually using at home and at work?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Andrea Mata

Dr. Andrea Mata (Dr. Dre) is a clinical psychologist, national speaker, and founder of BrightSpot Families. She's the author of The Number Two Parenting Book and graduated summa cum laude from Valparaiso University with her doctorate from Kent State.
